Issues.Title: Use System.AccessToken for internal feed auth on release/9.0
Issues.Body:
Restores internal feed authentication on `internal/release/9.0` after the `dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw` PAT was removed from arcade's vault manifest and expired (1ES PAT Disable Policy), causing fleet-wide 401s on `darc-int-*` / `dotnet-*-internal` feeds.

PR #67853 removed the `legacyCredential` PAT entirely, relying solely on the `NuGetAuthenticate@1` credential provider. That is **insufficient**: the Arcade toolset bootstrap restore (`build.cmd` -> `tools.ps1` -> `restore.proj`) reads credentials directly from `NuGet.config` and does not consume the credential provider. Build 3024337 (commit 7ea898fd, which contains #67853) still failed at toolset restore with all internal feeds unreachable.

This PR passes the build identity's OAuth token via `legacyCredential: $(System.AccessToken)`, which `SetupNugetSources.ps1` writes into `NuGet.config` as a live, permissioned credential. The `internal Build Service (dnceng)` identity has collaborator/contributor on the affected feeds. This matches the release/8.0 approach (#67854) and the mechanism that worked prior to PAT expiry (a cleartext credential in config).

Tracks AB#11679.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Use System.AccessToken for internal feed auth on release/9.0" (#67859)

Issues.Title: Deprecate the Blazor WebAssembly DevServer package
Issues.Body:
The standalone Blazor WebAssembly DevServer is being retired in favor of the newer Gateway host. This change makes that transition explicit by marking the package as deprecated, directing consumers to Gateway, and surfacing a clear startup warning so the migration path is visible where the package is actually used.

- Package deprecation
  - Mark `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer` as deprecated in package metadata and document `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway` as the replacement.
  - Update `PACKAGE.md` to present Gateway as the recommended hosting path for standalone Blazor WebAssembly apps.

- Runtime guidance
  - Emit a deprecation warning from `blazor-devserver` at startup so developers see the migration guidance immediately.
  - Preserve the existing `UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` call with a targeted suppression comment so the DevServer project continues to build during the deprecation transition.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Deprecate the Blazor WebAssembly DevServer package" (#67862)

Issues.Title: Deprecate UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor templates
Issues.Body:
# Deprecate UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor templates

<!-- Thank you for submitting a pull request to our repo. -->

<!-- If this is your first PR in the ASP.NET Core repo, please run through the checklist
below to ensure a smooth review and merge process for your PR. -->

- [ ] You've read the [Contributor Guide](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CODE-OF-CONDUCT.md).
- [ ] You've included unit or integration tests for your change, where applicable.
- [ ] You've included inline docs for your change, where applicable.
- [ ] There's an open issue for the PR that you are making. If you'd like to propose a new feature or change, please open an issue to discuss the change or find an existing issue.

<!-- Once all that is done, you're ready to go. Open the PR with the content below. -->

Deprecate UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove template emission

## Description

This change retires the legacy middleware-based WebAssembly debugging path from generated Blazor apps and aligns the API surface with the current IDE-launched debugger experience.

- Marked `UseWebAssemblyDebugging` as obsolete with `ASPDEPR011` and updated the shared deprecation metadata and public API baseline.
- Removed the middleware call from the Blazor Web App templates and simplified the development-only pipeline conditionals that were only there to support it.
- Suppressed the internal dev-server caller so the repository continues to build while preserving its compatibility path.
- Added regression coverage to ensure the templates no longer emit `UseWebAssemblyDebugging`.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Deprecate UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor templates" (#67861)

Issues.Title: Obsolete UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor templates
Issues.Body:
## Why

Blazor WebAssembly debugging is now launched directly by Visual Studio and Visual Studio Code, which start the debug proxy themselves. The legacy `UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` middleware only exists to support the old in-browser (Ctrl+Alt+D) experience, and usage of that path is very low. This deprecates the API and stops emitting it from our shipping templates.

Note: this means the legacy in-browser debugging path (including Firefox / non-VS scenarios) is no longer supported going forward.

## What changed

- **Obsoleted the API** as `ASPDEPR011`. Added the message, diagnostic id, and a new `AspNetCoreDeprecate011Url` entry to the shared `Obsoletions.cs`, applied `[Obsolete(...)]` to `WebAssemblyNetDebugProxyAppBuilderExtensions.UseWebAssemblyDebugging`, and wired the shared source into the WebAssembly.Server project.
- **Removed it from the Blazor Web App template** (`Program.cs` and `Program.Main.cs`) and collapsed the now-redundant `#if (UseWebAssembly || IndividualLocalAuth)` / `#if (UseWebAssembly)` conditionals down to `#if (IndividualLocalAuth)`.
- **Kept the repo building** by suppressing `ASPDEPR011` (with justifying comments) at the internal callers that still host the legacy proxy: the DevServer `Startup.cs`, plus the `HostedInAspNet.Server`, `Wasm.Prerendered.Server`, `HostedBlazorWebassemblyApp`, and Components `TestApp` assets.
- **Updated the WebAssembly.Server `PACKAGE.md`** sample so the docs match the templates.

## Notes for reviewers

- No `PublicAPI.*.txt` changes are needed. Marking a member `[Obsolete]` does not change the API signature text, consistent with the recent `ITlsTokenBindingFeature` deprecation (`ASPDEPR010`).
- The standalone Blazor WebAssembly template (`ComponentsWebAssembly-CSharp`) is client-only and needed no change.
- Verified locally: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.Server` and `...DevServer` both build with 0 warnings / 0 errors under warnings-as-errors, confirming the obsoletion and suppressions are correct.

Fixes #67858
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Obsolete UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor templates" (#67860)

Issues.Title: Obsolete UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor project templates
Issues.Body:
## Summary
Mark the `IApplicationBuilder.UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` extension (in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.Server`) as obsolete and stop emitting it from our project templates. Modern IDEs (Visual Studio, VS Code) launch the debug proxy directly. This does mean that the legacy in-browser (Ctrl+Alt+D) will no longer be supported.

## Background
- `UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` is defined in `src/Components/WebAssembly/Server/src/WebAssemblyNetDebugProxyAppBuilderExtensions.cs`. It maps `/_framework/debug` and uses `DebugProxyLauncher` to start the browser debug proxy (`BlazorDebugProxy/BrowserDebugHost.dll`) for the legacy Chromium/Firefox in-browser experience.
- Related configuration that only exists to support this path:
  - The `inspectUri` value in `launchSettings.json` (`.../_framework/debug/ws-proxy?browser={browserInspectUri}`). This is present in test assets but **not** in the shipping templates.
  - `NO_PROXY` handling and the `/_framework/debug` endpoint, both internal to the middleware/launcher.
- The IDE-driven debugger launches the debug proxy directly and does not depend on this middleware.

## Customer impact
Usage of the legacy in-browser debugging path appears low. The strongest signal is #61559 (Apr 2025), where browser debugging was broken out of the box on recent Chromium versions for roughly a month; it drew only 11 distinct commenters and no upvotes, and overall issue volume for this path is very small. We may still receive some complaints from users who rely on browser-only debugging (e.g. without Visual Studio, or in Firefox), so we should be sensitive to that in messaging.

## Proposed changes
1. **Obsolete the API** — Add `[Obsolete]` to `WebAssemblyNetDebugProxyAppBuilderExtensions.UseWebAssemblyDebugging`, using the next diagnostic ID (`ASPDEPR011`) and a new `AspNetCoreDeprecate011Url` entry in `src/Shared/Obsoletions.cs`. Update the public API baseline files (`PublicAPI.Shipped.txt` / `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt`).

   Obsoletion message:
   > `"UseWebAssemblyDebugging is obsolete and should be removed along with any inspectUri configurations. Blazor WebAssembly debugging is now launched directly by Visual Studio and Visual Studio Code."`

2. **Remove from the Blazor Web App template** — Remove `app.UseWebAssemblyDebugging();` (guarded by `#if (UseWebAssembly)`) from:
   - `src/ProjectTemplates/Web.ProjectTemplates/content/BlazorWeb-CSharp/BlazorWebCSharp.1/Program.cs`
   - `src/ProjectTemplates/Web.ProjectTemplates/content/BlazorWeb-CSharp/BlazorWebCSharp.1/Program.Main.cs`

   Collapse the now-redundant `#if (UseWebAssembly || IndividualLocalAuth)` / `#if (UseWebAssembly)` conditionals accordingly, and update template baseline/snapshot tests.

3. **Standalone Blazor WebAssembly template** — Verified no change needed: `ComponentsWebAssembly-CSharp` is client-only and does not reference `UseWebAssemblyDebugging` or `inspectUri`.

4. **Resolve the internal caller** — `src/Components/WebAssembly/DevServer/src/Server/Startup.cs` calls `UseWebAssemblyDebugging()`. Suppress the new obsoletion warning there (with a justifying comment) so the repo continues to build.

## Related work
- Companion issue: Deprecate the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer` package — #67857.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Obsolete UseWebAssemblyDebugging and remove it from Blazor project templates" (#67858)

Issues.Title: Deprecate the Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer package
Issues.Body:
## Summary
Mark the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer` NuGet package (assembly `blazor-devserver`) as deprecated. It is replaced by the new **Blazor Gateway** (`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway`, assembly `blazor-gateway`), which is the new server for hosting standalone Blazor WebAssembly applications during both development and production. Standalone WASM projects should move to the Gateway; the DevServer is no longer the intended hosting/debugging path.

## Background
- The DevServer package is produced from `src/Components/WebAssembly/DevServer/src/Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer.csproj` (`PackageId` `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer`, exe assembly `blazor-devserver`, description "Development server for use when building Blazor applications").
- Its replacement, the Blazor Gateway, lives at `src/Components/Gateway/src/Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway.csproj` (`PackageId` `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway`, exe assembly `blazor-gateway`, description "Gateway server for hosting standalone Blazor WebAssembly applications").
- The DevServer's `Startup.Configure` calls `app.UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` (`src/Components/WebAssembly/DevServer/src/Server/Startup.cs:32`) — the legacy in-browser debugging middleware being obsoleted in the companion issue.
- The DevServer package is consumed by **standalone Blazor WebAssembly** projects, where the `PackageReference` (with `PrivateAssets="all"`) is injected by the Blazor WebAssembly SDK rather than declared in the template. `PACKAGE.md` documents this and points to the Blazor debugging docs.

## Proposed changes
1. **Mark the package deprecated** — Add a deprecation notice to the package pointing users to the Blazor Gateway (`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway`) as the replacement, including an alternate-package pointer to `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway`, and update `PACKAGE.md`.
2. **Emit a startup warning** — Have `blazor-devserver` write a deprecation warning on startup stating that the DevServer is deprecated and that projects should move to the Blazor Gateway (`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway`).
3. **Update the Blazor WebAssembly SDK to stop injecting the DevServer** — In the Blazor WebAssembly SDK targets (`Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly`, in `dotnet/sdk`) that inject the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer` `PackageReference` and configure `dotnet run` to launch `blazor-devserver`, replace that with the Blazor Gateway (`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Gateway` / `blazor-gateway`) so standalone WASM projects run and debug against the Gateway.
4. **Resolve the internal `UseWebAssemblyDebugging` caller** — Suppress the new obsoletion warning for `app.UseWebAssemblyDebugging()` in `DevServer/src/Server/Startup.cs` (with a justifying comment referencing this deprecation) so the repo continues to build while the DevServer is on its way out.

## Related work
- Companion issue: Obsolete `UseWebAssemblyDebugging` and remove it from Blazor project templates — #67858.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Deprecate the Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.WebAssembly.DevServer package" (#67857)

Issues.Title: Set up a one-off publishing pipeline for npm packages using ESRP
Issues.Body:
Set up a one-off, manually-triggered pipeline to (re)publish the npm packages produced by an aspnetcore build to npmjs.org using ESRP Package Release.

The pipeline takes the BAR (Build Asset Registry / `darc`) build ID of an aspnetcore build as input, uses `darc gather-drop` to download that build's blob artifacts (npm `.tgz` packages are published as blob assets under `npm/`), stages the `.tgz` files, and hands them to the ESRP Release task (`EsrpRelease@11`, `Intent: PackageDistribution`, `ContentType: npm`) which signs and publishes them to `https://registry.npmjs.org/`. It extends the 1ES Official pipeline template, matching the pattern in `.azure/pipelines/ci.yml`.

There is no existing ESRP-based npm publishing precedent in the dotnet org (npm publishing to npmjs is currently a manual `npm publish` step per `dotnet/aspnetcore-internal/docs/release/AspNetSignalRBuildAndRelease.md`); the task schema is modeled on the Azure SDK / microsoft-mcp ESRP npm templates.

## TODOs before this can publish

The ESRP identity/onboarding values are exposed as pipeline parameters with clearly-marked `TODO-…` placeholder defaults. They must be filled in with the values assigned to aspnetcore during **ESRP Package Release onboarding** before the pipeline can publish:

- [ ] `esrpConnectedServiceName` — ESRP managed-identity ADO service connection name
- [ ] `esrpClientId` — managed-identity client id
- [ ] `esrpDomainTenantId` — domain tenant id (defaulted to the Microsoft tenant; confirm)
- [ ] `esrpKeyVaultName` — Key Vault holding the release signing certificate
- [ ] `esrpSignCertName` — signing certificate name
- [ ] `esrpMainPublisher` — ESRP main publisher id (start with the PPE/test publisher, then switch to prod)

Onboarding steps that produce these values (see the PRSS/ESRP SharePoint docs "Steps for onboarding to ESRP Release & Secure Origin" and "Integrating the ESRP Package Release ADO task"):

- [ ] File the ESRP onboarding request; loop in dnceng/DDFUN (may already have a managed identity + Key Vault from ESRP code signing that can be reused).
- [ ] Designate/allow-list a User-Assigned Managed Identity and create the ADO service connection bound to it.
- [ ] Register/point to the signing certificate + Key Vault and grant the identity read access.
- [ ] Get a publisher id assigned (test first, then prod).
- [ ] Complete "Secure Origin" registration for `registry.npmjs.org` and add ESRP's automation identity as an owner on the `@microsoft`/`@dotnet` npm orgs (coordinate with `aspnetnpmers`).
- [ ] Validate against ESRP PPE with a throwaway build, then flip to the production publisher.

Also worth confirming during review: whether ESRP prefers this modeled as a 1ES `releaseJob` deployment (as the Azure SDK examples do for production npmjs pushes) vs. the plain job used here, and whether `System.AccessToken` on the internal pool can reach the target build's artifacts if it's a cross-project build.

Opening as **draft** to preserve the work while onboarding is completed. No PRs/issues are otherwise affected.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Set up a one-off publishing pipeline for npm packages using ESRP" (#67852)

Issues.Title: Use build identity (System.AccessToken) for internal feed creds instead of expired dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw PAT (release/8.0)
Issues.Body:
## Summary
Internal official builds fail restore with **HTTP 401 on `darc-int-*` / `dotnet-*-internal` feeds** because `dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw` was removed from the arcade secret manifest (arcade #17079) and **expired 2026-07-11**.

## Change
The 8.0 `SetupNugetSources.ps1` has a **mandatory** `-Password`, and it is also the step that **re-enables the `darc-int` sources** (which ship disabled in `NuGet.config` via `<disabledPackageSources>`). So dropping it and relying on `NuGetAuthenticate@1` alone would **not** work — the sources would stay disabled. Instead this keeps the `Setup Private Feeds Credentials` task and swaps the credential from the dead PAT to the pipeline's **build-service OAuth token** `$(System.AccessToken)`.

`$(System.AccessToken)` is the dnceng/internal **Project Build Service** identity, which has read access to all affected feeds — this is the intended "build identity instead of PAT" migration for feeds hosted in the same AzDO org.

## Validation
- `SetupNugetSources` still enables the disabled `darc-int` sources.
- Build-service identity has ≥reader on `darc-int-*` / `dotnet-*-internal`.

Tracking: dnceng/internal AB#11679.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Use build identity (System.AccessToken) for internal feed creds instead of expired dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw PAT (release/8.0)" (#67854)

Issues.Title: Use build identity for internal feeds instead of expired dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw PAT (release/9.0)
Issues.Body:
## Summary
Internal official builds on this servicing branch are failing package restore with **HTTP 401 on all `darc-int-*` and `dotnet-*-internal` feeds** (e.g. build 3022753). Root cause: the `dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw` PAT was removed from the arcade secret manifest (arcade PR #17079, 2026-07-08) and **expired 2026-07-11**; rotation had stopped, so `legacyCredential: $(dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw)` now injects an expired/empty token.

## Change
Remove the `legacyCredential` PAT parameter from the `enable-internal-sources` step. For the `internal` project the template then takes the build-identity path (`SetupNugetSources` without a password + `NuGetAuthenticate@1`), authenticating internal feeds via the pipeline's build service identity. This matches how `main` already works.

## Validation
- dnceng/internal **Project Build Service** has ≥reader on the affected feeds: `contributor` on `darc-int-*`, `collaborator` on `dotnet9-internal`, `contributor` on `dotnet8-internal`.
- aspnetcore `main` builds restore internal feeds successfully with no PAT.

Tracking: dnceng/internal AB#11679 (live-site AB#11678).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Use build identity for internal feeds instead of expired dn-bot-dnceng-artifact-feeds-rw PAT (release/9.0)" (#67853)

Issues.Title: ProducesResponseTypeAttribute<T> / ServiceFilterAttribute<T> / TypeFilterAttribute<T> silently lose AllowMultiple across controller inheritance — they don't redeclare [AttributeUsage]
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

The generic attribute variants derive from their non-generic bases without redeclaring `[AttributeUsage]`:

| Attribute (Mvc.Core 10.0.10) | Declares own `[AttributeUsage]`? |
|---|---|
| `ProducesResponseTypeAttribute` | yes — `AllowMultiple = true` |
| `ProducesResponseTypeAttribute<T>` | no — inherits from base |
| `ServiceFilterAttribute` | yes — `AllowMultiple = true` |
| `ServiceFilterAttribute<T>` | no |
| `TypeFilterAttribute` | yes — `AllowMultiple = true` |
| `TypeFilterAttribute<T>` | no |

The C# compiler and plain attribute reflection both honor the *inherited* usage (`AllowMultiple = true` — two `[ProducesResponseType<T>]` on one action compile and reflect fine). But the runtime's `GetCustomAttributes(inherit: true)` **hierarchy walk** resolves `AttributeUsage` only from the attribute type's own metadata and falls back to `AttributeUsageAttribute.Default` (`AllowMultiple = false`) when none is declared. MVC's application model uses exactly that call to collect controller-level attributes, so when a controller *hierarchy* declares a generic variant at more than one level, only the most-derived instance survives:

- `[ProducesResponseType<T>]` on layered base controllers: ApiExplorer/OpenAPI silently drop the base levels — e.g. a shared `500` on an API base class vanishes from every endpoint as soon as a nearer base declares its own `403`.
- `[ServiceFilter<T>]` / `[TypeFilter<T>]`: worse — the base class's **filter silently stops executing** once a derived class declares one of its own. A behavioral bug, not just metadata.

The non-generic forms are unaffected because they carry their own `[AttributeUsage(..., AllowMultiple = true)]`.

### Expected Behavior

The generic and non-generic forms behave identically: instances from every level of the hierarchy are collected, consistent with the compiler accepting multiple instances and with the documented behavior of the non-generic attributes.

### Steps To Reproduce

Mechanism (single-file, `dotnet run` on .NET 10):

```csharp
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;

// generic variant — AttributeUsage inherited from base
Console.WriteLine("generic:     " + string.Join(", ",
    typeof(G2).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(ProducesResponseTypeAttribute), inherit: true)
        .Cast<ProducesResponseTypeAttribute>().Select(a => a.StatusCode)));
// actual:   403
// expected: 403, 401, 500

// non-generic — declares its own AttributeUsage
Console.WriteLine("non-generic: " + string.Join(", ",
    typeof(N2).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(ProducesResponseTypeAttribute), inherit: true)
        .Cast<ProducesResponseTypeAttribute>().Select(a => a.StatusCode)));
// prints:   403, 401, 500

[ProducesResponseType<ProblemDetails>(500)] class G0;
[ProducesResponseType<ProblemDetails>(401)] class G1 : G0;
[ProducesResponseType<ProblemDetails>(403)] class G2 : G1;

[ProducesResponseType(typeof(ProblemDetails), 500)] class N0;
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(ProblemDetails), 401)] class N1 : N0;
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(ProblemDetails), 403)] class N2 : N1;
```

End-to-end: put the same two chains behind MVC controllers (`Base(500) ← Authorized(401) ← Restricted(403) ← concrete controller`) and inspect `/openapi/v1.json` — the generic chain yields responses `200, 400, 403`; the non-generic chain yields `200, 400, 401, 403, 500`. The same shape with `[ServiceFilter<T>]` on two levels executes only the most-derived filter.

### Exceptions (if any)

None — the instances disappear silently.

### .NET Version

Reproduced on 10.0.10 (SDK 10.0.1xx). Present since the generic variants were introduced.

### Anything else?

The root cause is arguably the runtime's inheritance walk not honoring an *inherited* `AttributeUsage` (it disagrees with both the compiler and non-walk reflection), but that behavior is long-standing and presumably compat-frozen. Redeclaring the usage on the three generic variants fixes it completely and is binary-compatible:

```csharp
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class | AttributeTargets.Method, AllowMultiple = true, Inherited = true)]
public class ProducesResponseTypeAttribute<T> : ProducesResponseTypeAttribute
```

Any third-party attribute deriving from these (or any) attribute bases without redeclaring `[AttributeUsage]` hits the same collapse, so a docs note in the generic-attributes guidance may also be warranted.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "ProducesResponseTypeAttribute<T> / ServiceFilterAttribute<T> / TypeFilterAttribute<T> silently lose AllowMultiple across controller inheritance — they don't redeclare [AttributeUsage]" (#67856)

Issues.Title: Fix SignalR TS client access token retry when HttpClient throws on 401
Issues.Body:
Fix SignalR TypeScript client access token retry when the HttpClient throws on 401

- [x] You've read the [Contributor Guide](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CODE-OF-CONDUCT.md).
- [x] You've included unit or integration tests for your change, where applicable.
- [x] You've included inline docs for your change, where applicable.
- [x] There's an open issue for the PR that you are making. If you'd like to propose a new feature or change, please open an issue to discuss the change or find an existing issue.

## Description

In the browser/TypeScript SignalR client, `AccessTokenHttpClient.send` contains retry logic that is meant to renew the access token via the access token factory and resend the request when a 401 Unauthorized is received. However, the `HttpClient` implementations (e.g. `FetchHttpClient`) **throw** an `HttpError` for non-2xx responses, so the `response.statusCode === 401` check after the `await` is never reached — the thrown error propagates instead and the token is never renewed.

This notably affects:

- **Server-Sent Events keep-alive pings** — when the bearer token expires and the ping gets a 401, the connection closes instead of renewing the token, and
- **LongPolling** — a 401 on a reissued poll takes the long close lifecycle instead of renewing the token and continuing to poll.

### What changed

`AccessTokenHttpClient.send` now catches a thrown `HttpError` with status code 401 (subject to the same `allowRetry` conditions as the existing logic), renews the access token via the factory, sets the new `Authorization` header, and resends the request **once**. The retried request is intentionally not guarded against another 401, so a failing retry surfaces to the caller rather than retrying indefinitely — matching the single-retry semantics of the existing non-throwing path, which is unchanged. The shared renewal logic is extracted into a private `_retryWithNewToken` helper used by both paths.

Behavior is unchanged for: negotiate requests (never retried), clients without an access token factory, and non-401 errors (all propagate as before).

### Testing

Added `AccessTokenHttpClient.test.ts` with six tests:

- retries with a new token when the 401 arrives as a response (existing behavior, previously untested)
- retries with a new token when the 401 arrives as a thrown `HttpError` (the bug)
- retries at most once when the retried request also fails with 401
- does not retry on a thrown non-401 `HttpError`
- does not retry when no access token factory is configured
- does not retry negotiate requests

All SignalR TS client test suites that exercise this code path (`AccessTokenHttpClient`, `LongPollingTransport`, `ServerSentEventsTransport`, `HttpClient`, `HubConnection`) pass locally with this change.

Fixes #56494
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Fix SignalR TS client access token retry when HttpClient throws on 401" (#67851)

Issues.Title: DBSC: separate browser-advertised endpoint URLs from local handler paths
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem.

PR #67388 intentionally models `RegistrationPath` and `RefreshPath` as nonempty application-local `PathString` values. The handler owns those local request paths, prepends `Request.PathBase` when advertising them, and derives refresh-cookie scoping from the local refresh path. This is a safe, conformant server subset, but it cannot represent every URL reference allowed by DBSC.

The W3C DBSC protocol and Chromium support secure same-site absolute registration and refresh URLs. The current model therefore cannot cover a sibling endpoint origin, an alternate port, arbitrary public-to-local proxy path translation, or query-based routing.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Design separate browser-advertised registration and refresh URL-reference options while retaining local handler dispatch paths. Define precedence and consistency rules between the advertised references, local paths, `Request.PathBase`, and refresh-cookie path.

The design should address:

- HTTPS with an explicit localhost development exception, and rejection of user-info and fragments.
- Validation of every redirect hop before forwarding DBSC proof, session, or cookie data.
- Same-site checks using Public Suffix List/registrable-domain semantics.
- Cookie Domain, Path, Secure, and SameSite applicability across the advertised topology.
- Accepted Host restrictions and trusted forwarded scheme, host, and prefix configuration.
- A non-permissive CORS posture; endpoint reachability must not imply credentialed cross-origin API access.
- Interaction with `IncludeSite`, an explicit scope origin, `/.well-known/device-bound-sessions`, and Data Protection/ticket sharing between applications.
- Compatibility and migration behavior for the existing local `PathString` options.

Required coverage should include same-origin absolute URLs, secure same-site sibling origins, alternate ports, proxy-translated paths, query routing, invalid/cross-site/insecure destinations, redirects that change site or security, cookie applicability, PathBase behavior, host filtering, trusted forwarding, and multi-application Data Protection. The design requires a security/threat review before implementation.

This is related to, but separate from, #67827: advertised endpoint location controls where protocol requests are sent, while that issue controls subdomain registration requesting registrable-domain-root site scope. See also PR #67388.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "DBSC: separate browser-advertised endpoint URLs from local handler paths" (#67850)

Issues.Title: Treat QUERY as a safe HTTP method for antiforgery and CSRF protection
Issues.Body:
Fixes #67825

## Summary

The CSRF protection middleware (new in .NET 11) and the antiforgery middleware only treat the classic safe methods (GET/HEAD/OPTIONS/TRACE) as exempt from cross-origin / antiforgery validation. The `QUERY` HTTP method — added to `HttpMethods` in #63260 — was missing from both lists, so it was incorrectly treated as unsafe.

`QUERY` is defined as a **safe, idempotent** method by [RFC 10008 (The HTTP QUERY Method)](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc10008.html), so per its defined semantics it does not request any state change and belongs alongside GET/HEAD.

## Changes

- `DefaultCsrfProtection`: add `HttpMethods.Query` to the `SafeMethods` set.
- `DefaultAntiforgery.IsRequestValidAsync`: skip validation for `QUERY`.
- Added `QUERY` (and lowercase) cases to both existing safe-method test theories.

## Verification

- `DefaultCsrfProtectionTests.SafeMethods_AlwaysAllowed`: 8/8 passing.
- `DefaultAntiforgeryTest.IsRequestValidAsync_SkipsAntiforgery_ForSafeHttpMethods`: 6/6 passing.

## Note

For reference, Go's `net/http.CrossOriginProtection` (Go 1.25) currently exempts only GET/HEAD/OPTIONS and has not yet added QUERY.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Treat QUERY as a safe HTTP method for antiforgery and CSRF protection" (#67839)

Issues.Title: Kestrel does not validate the query component of HTTP/2/3 :path, allowing raw whitespace/control chars
Issues.Body: Kestrel validates only the path portion of the HTTP/2 (and HTTP/3) `:path` pseudo-header; everything after `?` is stored into `QueryString/RawTarget` unvalidated (`Http2Stream.TryValidatePath` operates on `pathSegment` before `queryIndex`). This lets a raw space or other control characters survive in the query. Kestrel should reject or consistently handle disallowed characters in the query component to match path validation
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Kestrel does not validate the query component of HTTP/2/3 :path, allowing raw whitespace/control chars" (#67849)

Issues.Title: Design Proposal: Footer Templates for QuickGrid and Its Columns
Issues.Body:
<!--
This template is useful to build consensus about whether work should be done, and if so, the high-level shape of how it should be approached. Use this before fixating on a particular implementation.
-->

## Summary

QuickGrid previously had no way to render footer content, so users could not display column aggregates (such as grand totals) or full-width summary rows. This change adds two optional render-fragment parameters: a grid-level `FooterTemplate` that spans all columns and a per-column `FooterTemplate` for individual footer cells. When either is supplied, QuickGrid emits a `<tfoot>` section; otherwise the grid renders exactly as before.

## Motivation and Goals

- Address the requests in issues [QuickGrid footer missing · Issue #54895 · dotnet/aspnetcore](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/54895) and [Footer in QuickGrid · Issue #58711 · dotnet/aspnetcore](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/58711) for a QuickGrid footer, specifically to show a grand total of a column and to provide a general footer area.
- Provide per-column footer cells (`ColumnBase.FooterTemplate`) for column-scoped aggregates such as sums, averages, min/max, and counts.
- Provide a grid-level footer (`QuickGrid.FooterTemplate`) for full-width content such as pagination summaries, grand totals combining columns, or action/disclaimer text.
- Keep the feature opt-in so existing grids without templates render no additional markup.

## In Scope

- New parameter `QuickGrid<TGridItem>.FooterTemplate` rendered inside a `<tfoot>` row as a single `<td>` with `colspan` equal to the column count (`grid-footer-cell` class).
- New parameter `ColumnBase<TGridItem>.FooterTemplate` rendered as a per-column `<td>` in a `<tfoot>` row (`grid-column-footer-cell` class).
- Rendering logic that emits a `<tfoot>` only when at least one column has a footer template or a grid footer is set, plus an updated `aria-rowcount` that accounts for the footer rows.
- CSS additions for the footer cells and a new test file (`QuickGridFooterTemplateTest.cs`) plus `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` entries.

## Out of Scope

- Automatic aggregation helpers (sum/average/count) — the templates render arbitrary content but compute nothing on their own.
- Sort-aware footer values computed automatically; reflecting the current sort order requires the user to capture data via `ItemsProvider`.
- Sticky/pinned footer positioning behavior or scrolling-specific footer handling.
- None identified beyond the above from the Issue and PR analysis.

## Risks and Unknowns

- Footer content is not automatically recalculated against the current sorted/paged view; users must wire aggregates through `ItemsProvider`, which may be a source of confusion.
- The grid-level footer uses `colspan` equal to the current column count, so dynamic column changes rely on re-rendering to keep the span correct.
- When only some columns define `FooterTemplate`, empty footer cells are still rendered for the remaining columns, which affects footer row layout.
- None identified regarding interaction with virtualization from the Issue and PR analysis.

## API Surface

- `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.QuickGrid<TGridItem>.FooterTemplate.get/set -> Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.RenderFragment?`
- `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.ColumnBase<TGridItem>.FooterTemplate.get/set -> Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.RenderFragment?`
- Both are added as `[Parameter]` properties and recorded in `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt`. No existing APIs are removed or changed.

### Accessibility

Footers use semantic `<tfoot>`/`<td>` elements and `aria-rowcount` is updated to include the rendered footer rows, so assistive technologies report the correct total row count.

## Edge Case Scenarios

- **No template set:** no `<tfoot>` element is rendered.
- **Grid or column footer toggled to `null` on re-render:** the `<tfoot>` (or that footer content) is removed.
- **Empty item set:** footer still renders (e.g., a "No items" or "Total: 0" footer).
- **Partial column footers:** a footer row renders for all columns, with empty cells for columns lacking a template; both column-level and grid-level footers can render together.
- **Pagination or sortable columns present:** footer renders normally, and sort-aware values are produced only when data is captured through `ItemsProvider`.

## Examples

```razor
<QuickGrid Items="@people">
    <PropertyColumn Property="@(p => p.Name)" Title="Name">
        <FooterTemplate>Total: @people.Count()</FooterTemplate>
    </PropertyColumn>
    <PropertyColumn Property="@(p => p.Salary)" Title="Salary">
        <FooterTemplate>Sum: @people.Sum(p => p.Salary)</FooterTemplate>
    </PropertyColumn>

    <FooterTemplate>
        Showing @people.Count() results
    </FooterTemplate>
</QuickGrid>
```
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design Proposal: Footer Templates for QuickGrid and Its Columns" (#67848)

Issues.Title: Design Proposal: Provide a JavaScript Signal for Interactive Rendering
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Introduce a new JavaScript event, `afterAllRenderBatchesApplied`, that is exposed through the existing Blazor event infrastructure.

The event is dispatched after `Renderer.renderBatch(...)` finishes applying a render batch and can be consumed through `Blazor.addEventListener(...)`.

## Motivation and goals

Issue [[Blazor] E2E testing improvements (#65420)](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/65420) requests a JavaScript-accessible signal that can be used to determine when rendering associated with interactive components has completed.

There is no renderer-level notification exposed through the Blazor JavaScript APIs. Consumers typically rely on framework implementation details such as DOM inspection, element metadata, or polling to infer when rendering has completed.

For example, E2E tests often need to inspect generated `_blazorEvents_*` properties or wait for framework-specific DOM state before interacting with components.

This proposal introduces a notification directly from the rendering pipeline and exposes it through the existing `JSEventRegistry` infrastructure.

## In scope

- Add a new Blazor JavaScript event named `afterAllRenderBatchesApplied`.
- Dispatch the event from the shared rendering pipeline.
- Expose the event through the existing event registration APIs.
- Support Blazor Server, Blazor WebAssembly, and Blazor Web Apps.
- Add E2E coverage validating event dispatch and listener removal behavior.

## Out of scope

- Component-level render notifications.
- Component-specific interactivity events.
- Event payloads containing render batch data.
- New JavaScript subscription APIs.
- Distinguishing between initial rendering and subsequent render batches.

## Risks / unknowns

### Event meaning

The event represents completion of render batch processing.

It does not indicate:

- Application startup completion.
- Completion of component initialization.
- Completion of asynchronous operations scheduled by components.

### Event frequency

The event is dispatched after every render batch. Consumers must not assume it represents a one-time transition.

### Future rendering changes

The event contract is tied to render batch completion. Future rendering changes must preserve equivalent behavior for existing subscribers.

## Examples

```javascript
Blazor.addEventListener(
    'afterAllRenderBatchesApplied',
    () => {
        console.log('Render batch completed');
    });
```

```javascript
await new Promise(resolve =>
    Blazor.addEventListener(
        'afterAllRenderBatchesApplied',
        resolve,
        { once: true }));
```

# Detailed design

## Event registration

A new entry is added to `BlazorEventMap` in:

```text
src/Components/Web.JS/src/Services/JSEventRegistry.ts
```

```ts
'afterAllRenderBatchesApplied': BlazorEvent,
```

No additional event infrastructure is introduced.

The event becomes available through the existing:

```javascript
Blazor.addEventListener(...)
Blazor.removeEventListener(...)
```

APIs.

The event name reflects the renderer-level contract being exposed. The notification is tied to completion of render-batch application rather than component lifecycle transitions.

## Renderer integration

The event is dispatched from:

```text
src/Components/Web.JS/src/Rendering/Renderer.ts
```

A module-level reference to `JSEventRegistry` is introduced:

```ts
let jsEventRegistry: JSEventRegistry | undefined;
```

and initialized through:

```ts
export function setRenderBatchJSEventRegistry(
    registry: JSEventRegistry): void
```

This allows `Renderer.ts` to dispatch framework events without introducing a second event system.

## Dispatch point

The event is raised at the end of:

```ts
renderBatch(browserRendererId: number, batch: RenderBatch)
```

Current render processing completes before the event is dispatched:

```ts
resetScrollIfNeeded(ScrollResetSchedule.AfterBatch);
jsEventRegistry?.dispatchEvent('afterAllRenderBatchesApplied', {});
```

This location was selected because all interactive rendering paths ultimately flow through `renderBatch(...)`.

Raising the event from the renderer avoids hosting-model-specific logic and provides consistent behavior across Server, WebAssembly, and Web App hosting models.

The dispatch occurs after:

- render batch processing,
- DOM updates,
- scroll reset processing.

Dispatching earlier would expose intermediate rendering states where DOM updates may not yet be fully applied.

## Startup wiring

Because `Renderer.ts` does not create the event registry, the registry reference is provided during startup.

The following startup paths are updated:

```text
src/Components/Web.JS/src/Boot.Server.ts
src/Components/Web.JS/src/Boot.Web.ts
src/Components/Web.JS/src/Boot.WebAssembly.ts
```

Each startup path initializes a `JSEventRegistry` instance and provides it to the renderer through:

```ts
const jsEventRegistry = JSEventRegistry.create(Blazor);
setRenderBatchJSEventRegistry(jsEventRegistry);
```

The renderer does not create its own registry and continues to rely on the existing event infrastructure.

## Event semantics

The event is dispatched once for each completed invocation of:

```ts
renderBatch(...)
```

The event is renderer-scoped rather than component-scoped.

The event may be observed during:

- Initial interactive rendering.
- Component rerenders.
- Enhanced navigation updates.
- Any operation that produces a render batch.

The event does not distinguish between:

- Initial render.
- Interactive activation.
- Enhanced navigation updates.
- Component rerenders.

Consumers interested in determining when content becomes interactive are expected to observe the first relevant event for their scenario.

The only guarantee provided is that the render batch has been processed and applied before the event is dispatched.

## Validation

### Event dispatch

The following E2E test was added:

```text
AfterAllRenderBatchesApplied_EventFires_WhenServerComponentBecomesInteractive
```

The test registers a listener through:

```javascript
Blazor.addEventListener(
    'afterAllRenderBatchesApplied',
    ...)
```

and verifies that the listener is invoked after a render batch generated by user interaction.

### Listener removal

The following E2E test was added:

```text
AfterAllRenderBatchesApplied_EventFires_OncePerRenderBatch_AllowsRemoveEventListener
```

The test verifies:

- Listener registration.
- Listener removal.
- Removed listeners are not invoked.
- Active listeners continue receiving notifications.

### Test asset

A dedicated test page was added:

```text
src/Components/test/testassets/Components.TestServer/
RazorComponents/Pages/Interactivity/
AfterRenderBatchAppliedSignal.razor
```

The page hosts `ServerInteractiveCounter` and provides a deterministic render surface for validating event dispatch behavior.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design Proposal: Provide a JavaScript Signal for Interactive Rendering" (#67846)

Issues.Title: Design for Add RoutePrefix Parameter to Blazor Router for Localized URL Support
Issues.Body:
## Summary This proposal introduces a `RoutePrefix` parameter to the Blazor `Router` component that allows developers to define a path prefix pattern (such as a language/culture code) that is stripped before route matching. This enables URLs like `/en/home` to route to components declared with `@page "/home"` without duplicating the prefix in every component's route declaration. The feature supports parameterized prefixes with route constraints, making the prefix value available as a route parameter to components. ## Motivation and Goals [Adding feature to Router for custom language URL #40736](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/40736) highlights a significant gap in Blazor's routing capabilities. When developers want to support multi-language or multi-culture URLs like `/en/home`, `/de/home`, and `/es/home`, the URL structure becomes disconnected from the component route structure. The goal of this proposal is to make localized URL routing straightforward for Blazor applications by ensuring that: - **Enable localized URL patterns**: Support multi-language/multi-culture URL schemes where the culture is part of the URL path without requiring each component to declare the prefix in its `@page` directive. - **Reduce routing boilerplate**: Eliminate the need to create multiple page variants or complicated workarounds for culture-based URL prefixes. - **Maintain backward compatibility**: Ensure existing applications continue to work without any changes to the `Router` component. - **Provide route parameter access**: Allow components to access the prefix parameters (e.g., culture code) as route parameters for use within the application. ## In Scope - Add a `RoutePrefix` parameter to the `Router` component that accepts a route template string (e.g., `/{culture:regex(^[a-z]{2}$)}`). - Support route constraints and parameter policies in the prefix template (e.g., `regex`, `int`, `bool`). - Strip the matched prefix from the URL before component route matching. - Merge prefix route parameters with component route parameters in the resulting `RouteData`. - Support optional and catch-all segments in the prefix template where applicable. - Make the prefix-matched values available as route parameters to matched components. - Support both Blazor Server and Blazor WebAssembly routing scenarios. ## Out of Scope - Server-side Razor Pages routing or MVC routing modifications. - Changes to the `NavigationManager` for URL generation with prefix awareness. - Automatic culture detection or CultureInfo configuration based on matched prefix values. - Multiple prefix patterns or conditional prefix matching. - Changes to existing link generation APIs or tag helpers for building prefixed links. ## Risks and Unknowns - **Performance impact**: Route matching now involves prefix matching before component matching; ensure performance is acceptable even with complex prefix patterns. - **Ambiguous routes**: If a component route can match a prefix pattern, it could cause routing ambiguity; detection and clear error messaging are needed. - **Parameter conversion**: Complex parameter types in prefix templates must be correctly converted (e.g., `{id:int}`) to match the component routing behavior. - **Query strings and fragments**: Ensure that query strings and URL fragments following the prefix are handled correctly. - **Dynamic prefix changes**: Behavior is undefined if the `RoutePrefix` parameter changes after component initialization; documentation should clarify that it is not supported. ## API Surface ### New Parameter on Router Component ```csharp /// /// Gets or sets the route prefix template that should be stripped from the path before component matching. /// The template is parsed as a route pattern and can include route constraints (e.g., /{culture:regex(^[a-z]{2}$)}). /// If the current path matches this prefix, the prefix is removed and prefix route parameters are added to the component's route data. /// If the path does not match the prefix, the route will not match any component. /// [Parameter] public string? RoutePrefix { get; set; } ``` ### Affected Classes - `Router`: Main component that receives the new parameter. - `Router.Refresh()`: Internal method updated to handle prefix matching before component route matching. - Public API surface: Single new component parameter. ## Edge Case Scenarios - **No prefix in URL**: If `RoutePrefix` is set to `/{culture}` and a URL like `/home` (without culture) is navigated to, the route will not match, resulting in a Not Found response. - **Ambiguous prefix and component routes**: If both a prefix template and a component route could match the same URL segment (e.g., prefix `/{id:int}` and component `@page "/{id:int}"`), the behavior must be clearly documented and tested. - **Case-sensitive matching**: Route pattern matching in .NET routing is case-insensitive by default; ensure this is consistent with prefix matching. - **Prefix with optional segments**: If the prefix is `/{culture?}` (optional), both `/en/home` and `/home` should be valid; requires careful implementation. - **Empty path after prefix removal**: If the URL is exactly `/en` and the prefix is `/{culture}`, the remaining path is empty; component routes should handle this gracefully (e.g., mapped to `/` for index components). - **Prefix with catch-all**: If the prefix is `/{*path}`, it could consume the entire URL; this should be clearly discouraged or unsupported in documentation. ## Examples ### Basic Usage: Culture-Based URL Prefix **Scenario**: An application wants to support URLs like `/en/home`, `/de/home`, `/es/home` where the culture is part of the URL. **Routes.razor** (Router component with prefix): ```razor @using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Routing Not found

Sorry, there's nothing at this address.

 ``` **Home.razor** (Component receiving the culture parameter): ```razor @page "/home" @{ var culture = RouteData?.RouteValues["culture"]?.ToString() ?? "unknown"; }

Welcome to @culture Home

Current culture from URL: @culture

@code { [Parameter] public RouteData? RouteData { get; set; } } ``` **Navigation behavior**: - `/en/home` matches prefix `{culture=en}`, remaining path `/home` matches component route, component receives `culture="en"` as a route parameter - `/de/home` matches prefix `{culture=de}`, remaining path `/home` matches component route, component receives `culture="de"` as a route parameter - `/home` does not match prefix (requires 2-letter code), results in Not Found - `/en-us/home` does not match prefix (regex requires exactly 2 letters), results in Not Found ### Detailed Design The `RoutePrefix` parameter is consumed once during the `Refresh()` method on the `Router` component. The implementation parses the prefix as a `RoutePattern` and constructs a synthetic `RouteTable` with a single entry that uses a sentinel handler type (`_RoutePrefix`). When the URL is matched against this prefix table: 1. The current relative path is first routed through the prefix `RouteTable`. 2. If `context.Handler == typeof(_RoutePrefix)`, the prefix matched. The matched prefix segments are stripped from the path, and the resulting route values are added to the component-level `RouteData`. 3. If the prefix does not match, the navigation is treated as Not Found (unless the prefix is null/empty, in which case the behavior is unchanged from existing routing). ### Key Implementation Points - **Prefix parsing**: Uses the same `RoutePatternParser` and constraint resolver pipeline as component routes, so constraints like `regex`, `int`, `bool`, `datetime`, `guid`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `decimal` work identically in the prefix. - **Path slicing**: After prefix matching, the matched literal portions of the prefix are removed from the URL to produce the residual path. The residual path is then routed against the component `RouteTable`. - **RouteData merging**: Prefix route values are prepended to the component `RouteData.RouteValues` so that components can access them via `RouteData.RouteValues["culture"]`. - **Caching**: The prefix `RouteTable` is built once and cached as long as the `RoutePrefix` parameter value does not change. A `RouteKey`-like equality check is used to invalidate the cache when the prefix template changes. ### Interaction with Endpoint Routing When `RoutingStateProvider.RouteData` is already supplied (the endpoint-routing / SSR case), the prefix is not applied because endpoint routing is expected to have already handled the culture segment at the middleware level. The prefix only applies to the in-browser routing case where the `Router` component is performing client-side matching. ## Drawbacks - **Extra route match per navigation**: Every navigation performs one additional route table lookup against the prefix. For applications with large numbers of routes, this is negligible, but it is a small added cost compared to the current single-pass matching. - **Ambiguity with component routes**: A prefix like `/{id:int}` could be confused with a component route like `@page "/{id:int}"`. The implementation relies on the sentinel handler type to disambiguate, but documentation must clearly state that the prefix consumes URL segments before component matching. - **No link generation awareness**: Because `NavigationManager` is unaware of the prefix, code that builds URLs manually (e.g., `navManager.NavigateTo("/home")` instead of `navManager.NavigateTo("/en/home")`) will not work unless the developer remembers to include the prefix. This is a usability gap that is explicitly Out of Scope. - **No built-in culture switching**: The prefix value is exposed as a route parameter, but the framework does not automatically configure `CultureInfo` based on it. Developers must read the route value and apply it themselves. ## Considered Alternatives - **Pass a delegate instead of a string template**: Rejected because the route template syntax is already familiar to Blazor developers and reuses the existing constraint infrastructure. A delegate API would require more documentation and is harder to express simple cases like culture codes. - **Subclass `Router` to override `Refresh`**: Rejected because the issue itself notes that "many of the classes are protected and inaccessible." Adding a first-class parameter is the more discoverable and maintainable solution. - **Server-side middleware redirect (the workaround in the issue)**: A common pattern in the issue comments is to redirect `/en/home` to `/home` via ASP.NET Core middleware and store the culture in a cookie. This works but loses the culture information in the URL on subsequent navigations. The `RoutePrefix` approach keeps the culture visible in the URL. - **Multiple prefix parameters (e.g., `/{lang}/{region}/...`)**: Supported by the template syntax naturally (e.g., `RoutePrefix="/{lang}/{region}"`), but explicitly documenting common single-parameter (culture) scenarios is the primary motivator. Multi-segment prefixes work but are not the headline use case. - **Automatic `CultureInfo` configuration**: Considered but rejected for v1 because it would require coupling the routing layer to `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Localization`. Exposing the value as a route parameter keeps the design decoupled and lets developers apply the culture however they prefer.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design for Add RoutePrefix Parameter to Blazor Router for Localized URL Support" (#67845)

Issues.Title: Ensure test coverage and correct behavior for OpenAPI when minimal API parameter is `T?[]`

In https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/pull/63301

Issues.Title: Proposal: Synchronize form reset behavior with EditContext and InputBase
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Blazor forms currently allow the browser to clear the DOM value of an input when a reset button is clicked, but the corresponding component state can remain out of sync. This is especially visible for bound input components, where the browser resets the visible field value while the underlying model or component state does not reflect that reset. This proposal synchronizes form reset behavior with `EditContext` and `InputBase` so that a reset event updates the component state consistently with the browser’s DOM reset behavior.

## Motivation and goals

[Synchronize form reset behavior with EditContext and InputBase (#21179)](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/21179) highlights a long-standing inconsistency in Blazor forms. When a user triggers a form reset, the browser updates the visible DOM state, but the bound component and model state may remain unchanged.

The goal of this proposal is to make reset behavior predictable for Blazor forms by ensuring that:

- a reset event raised by the form is observed by the form infrastructure,
- bound input components receive the reset notification,
- the component value is synchronized with the state produced by the browser reset operation,
- the form’s modification and validation state are updated so the UI reflects the reset.

This should be achieved without introducing a new form model or breaking existing form semantics.

### Non-goals

This proposal does not attempt to:

- change the browser’s native HTML reset behavior,
- introduce a new form-reset API for arbitrary custom components,
- alter submit behavior or validation execution semantics,
- change how forms are rendered outside the existing `EditForm`/`InputBase` pipeline.

## In scope

- Adding a reset-notification pathway from `EditForm` to `EditContext`.
- Allowing input components built on `InputBase` to react to form reset events.
- Synchronizing input component values with the state produced by the browser reset operation.
- Clearing validation state associated with the reset inputs so the UI remains consistent.
- Clearing modification state for fields in the form when a reset occurs.

## Out of scope

- Adding a new public API for arbitrary custom components beyond the reset event exposed on `EditContext`.
- Providing custom reset semantics for components that do not participate in the `InputBase`/`EditContext` model.
- Altering browser DOM behavior for non-Blazor form controls.
- Introducing new validation rules or validation providers.

## API proposal

The proposal introduces an additive reset-notification API on `EditContext`.

```
public sealed class EditContext
{
    public event EventHandler? OnReset;

    public void NotifyResetRequested();
}
```

### API shape rationale

This approach is chosen because the reset lifecycle belongs to the form infrastructure rather than to any one input component. By exposing the notification on `EditContext`, the feature can be raised once from the form container and then observed by all relevant inputs that participate in the same editing context.

This keeps the feature:

- additive,
- consistent with the existing `EditContext` model,
- easy for built-in components to consume,
- compatible with existing form usage.

## Alternative designs considered

### 1. Handle reset entirely in the browser via JavaScript

A JavaScript-based approach could observe the DOM reset event and push values back into Blazor state. This was rejected because it would require more invasive browser interop, would be harder to keep consistent across render modes, and would not naturally integrate with `EditContext` and component-level validation state.

### 2. Leave reset behavior to application code

Applications could manually handle reset events and reset their own model values. This would work for some cases, but it would require custom code in every scenario and would not provide a consistent default experience for built-in Blazor input components.

### 3. Add reset handling only to individual input components

This would be possible, but it would not provide a form-level coordination mechanism. The form reset event needs to propagate through the shared `EditContext` so that modification flags and validation state can be reset consistently across the form.

The proposed design is the most cohesive and least intrusive option because it uses the existing form and edit-context pipeline.

## Reset semantics

This proposal aligns reset behavior with Blazor's existing binding model.

Blazor's `@bind` infrastructure updates DOM properties rather than HTML attributes. As a result, browsers do not retain an original attribute-based value that can be restored during a reset operation.

When a reset occurs, browsers typically restore the input to an empty or default value. The proposed implementation propagates this reset through the Blazor binding pipeline so that component state and model state remain synchronized with the visible DOM state.

The goal is consistency between the UI and model rather than attempting to reconstruct an original value that is not reliably available through the existing binding model.

## Breaking changes and compatibility

This proposal does not introduce breaking changes.

- Existing form behavior remains unchanged unless a reset button is clicked.
- Existing `EditForm` and `InputBase` usage continues to work as before.
- Existing validation and modification state handling remains intact.
- The new behavior is additive and only affects the reset interaction path.

The only behavior change is that reset events now cause the bound form state to stay synchronized with the browser’s visible reset action.

## Performance considerations

The change introduces negligible overhead.

- The reset notification is only raised when a form reset event occurs.
- The cost of the notification path is small and proportional to the number of subscribed input components.
- No additional validation pass or background work is introduced.
- The change does not alter normal field updates or normal form submission behavior.

This makes the feature suitable for interactive form scenarios without affecting typical performance characteristics.

## Security considerations

The change presents low security risk.

- It does not introduce new data access paths.
- It does not bypass validation or authorization behavior.
- It only affects the local component state and edit-context lifecycle for reset interactions.

The main concern is correctness and consistency rather than security.

## Risks / unknowns

- Developers familiar with native HTML forms may expect reset to restore the original value attribute. Blazor's binding model does not preserve attribute-based values in a form that can be reliably restored.
- Components that do not participate in the `InputBase` / `EditContext` pipeline will not automatically respond to reset notifications.
- The notification cost scales with the number of participating inputs, although resets are expected to occur infrequently.
- Future component types may require additional reset integration if they maintain state outside the existing binding pipeline.

## Diagnostics and error handling

The proposal should be explicit and predictable.

### Expected behavior

- When a form reset is requested, the reset notification is raised through `EditContext`.
- Built-in input components synchronize their current value with the state produced by the browser reset operation.
- Validation state is cleared for the affected inputs so the UI no longer shows stale validation messages.
- Modification flags are cleared so the form is no longer marked as dirty after the reset.

If a component does not participate in `InputBase` or does not subscribe to the reset event, it will not automatically receive the reset notification. This is acceptable because the feature is implemented for the built-in input pipeline and can be adopted by custom components as needed.

## Testing strategy

The feature should be covered by focused tests that validate both the reset infrastructure and the user-visible behavior.

### Proposed test coverage

- validating that `EditForm` emits the reset event when the form receives a reset notification,
- validating that `EditContext` raises reset notifications and allows consumers to observe them,
- validating that `InputBase`-derived components synchronize correctly with the browser reset state,
- validating multiple-input scenarios,
- validating that validation messages are cleared after a reset,
- validating that modification state is cleared after a reset,
- validating reset behavior for both empty and non-empty initial states,
- validating reset notification subscription and propagation,
- validating reset behavior through the existing binding pipeline,
- validating reset behavior when validation messages are present,
- validating reset behavior after fields have been modified.

These tests should ensure that the reset path is reliable and that forms remain consistent after a reset interaction.

## Examples

### Example 1: Reset a single bound input

```
<EditForm EditContext="editContext">
    <InputText @bind-Value="model.Name" />
    <button type="reset">Reset</button>
</EditForm>
```

After clicking the reset button, the visible input value and the bound component value remain synchronized and reflect the reset state produced by the browser.

### Example 2: Reset a form with multiple bound fields

```
<EditForm EditContext="editContext">
    <InputText @bind-Value="model.FirstName" />
    <InputText @bind-Value="model.LastName" />
    <button type="reset">Reset</button>
</EditForm>
```

All bound fields should reset consistently when the form is reset.

## Detailed design

The proposed design builds on the existing `EditForm`/`EditContext`/`InputBase` architecture rather than replacing it.

### Implementation outline

- Add an `OnReset` event to `EditContext`.
- Add a `NotifyResetRequested()` method to `EditContext`.
- Update `EditForm` to subscribe to the browser’s `reset` event and call `EditContext.NotifyResetRequested()` when it occurs.
- Update `EditForm` to clear modification state and notify validation-state change after a reset request.
- Update `InputBase` to subscribe to `EditContext.OnReset`.
- When a reset notification is received, synchronize the input component's current value with the browser reset state and clear any parsing-side validation state.
- Notify the form validation pipeline so validation UI is updated.

### Internal behavior

- The browser triggers the form reset event.
- `EditForm` receives the event and raises a reset notification on `EditContext`.
- `EditContext` propagates the event to all interested input components.
- `InputBase`-derived components synchronize their value with the state produced by the browser reset operation.
- The form’s modification and validation state is updated to reflect the reset.
- Parsing validation messages maintained by `InputBase` are cleared.
- `EditContext.MarkAsUnmodified()` is invoked to clear modification state.
- `EditContext.NotifyValidationStateChanged()` is invoked so validation UI is refreshed.

This design preserves the existing Blazor form model while making reset behavior consistent and predictable.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Proposal: Synchronize form reset behavior with EditContext and InputBase" (#67843)
