Issues.Title: Design for Enhance nullability support for TValue in EventCallback
Issues.Body:
## Enhance nullability support for TValue in EventCallback

Tighten `EventCallback<TValue>.InvokeAsync(TValue)` nullability annotations so the method signature carries the declared nullability of `TValue`, restoring nullable-reference-type diagnostics and eliminating invasive null checks in callback handlers. #54502

## Motivation and goals

- Improve nullable-reference-type safety for EventCallback<TValue>.
- Ensure compiler nullability analysis respects whether TValue is nullable or non-nullable.
- Reduce situations where callbacks can be invoked with null despite a non-nullable type declaration.
- Eliminate unnecessary null-check warnings in callback handlers when TValue is declared as non-nullable.

## In scope

- Enhance nullability support and annotations for EventCallback<TValue>.
- Improve compiler diagnostics when invoking callbacks with values that do not match the declared nullability of TValue.
- Align callback invocation APIs with nullable-reference-type analysis.
- Improve developer experience for strongly typed Blazor event callbacks.

## Out of scope

- Runtime enforcement of nullability constraints beyond compiler analysis.
- Changes to callback execution behavior unrelated to nullability annotations.
- Changes to non-generic EventCallback.
- New event callback features unrelated to nullability.

## Risks / unknowns

- Consumers who have turned on nullable-reference-type warnings may see new warnings at call sites that were previously silent.
- The change only affects what the compiler reports; the generated code and runtime behavior stay exactly the same, so existing apps keep working.
- Some external tools (such as source generators or AOT tooling) read nullability metadata directly, and they may need a small update to recognize the new annotations.

## Edge Case Scenarios

- `EventCallback<string>` invoked with `null` is now flagged as a nullability mismatch.
- `EventCallback<string?>` invoked with `null` remains allowed.
- Handlers for non-nullable `TValue` no longer need defensive null checks.
- Generic components receive diagnostics consistent with the declared `TValue` nullability.

## API surface

```csharp
    /// <summary>
    /// Invokes the delegate associated with this binding and dispatches an event notification to the
    /// appropriate component.
    /// </summary>
    /// <param name="arg">The argument.</param>
    /// <returns>A <see cref="Task"/> which completes asynchronously once event processing has completed.</returns>
    public Task InvokeAsync(TValue arg)
```

## Examples

```razor
    EventCallback<string> callback = ...;

    // Produces nullable analysis diagnostics because TValue is non-nullable.
    await callback.InvokeAsync(null);

    EventCallback<string?> nullableCallback = ...;

    // Allowed because TValue is nullable.
    await nullableCallback.InvokeAsync(null);
```

The same change also removes the need for defensive null checks in the handler itself, since the compiler now knows the callback argument is non-nullable:

```csharp
// Without the fix: warning CS8602 "dereference of a possibly null reference".
async Task Handle(MyEvent e)
{
    Console.WriteLine(e.Value.Length);
}

EventCallback<MyEvent> cb = EventCallback.Factory.Create<MyEvent>(this, Handle);
await cb.InvokeAsync(new MyEvent("hello")); // No null guard required in Handle.
```

## Detailed design

- The parameter on `InvokeAsync` is re-annotated so that its nullability follows whatever nullability `TValue` was declared with, which lets the compiler report mismatches correctly.
- The change is purely a documentation/metadata change in the assembly the actual code that runs and the method signature callers see stay the same.
- The same fix is also applied to the `EventCallback<TValue>` constructor and the `EventCallback.Factory.Create<TValue>` helpers, so creating a callback does not accidentally lose the new nullability information.
- Because the change only updates metadata, it only needs to ship on the current target frameworks; older ones do not need a separate package.

## Drawbacks

- Projects that treat warnings as errors may stop building after the change, even though the code itself still runs correctly.
- Turning on nullable warnings across a large codebase can cause many unrelated files to start showing new warnings, because the framework is used in so many places.
- Once the annotations are made stricter, they cannot be loosened again later without formally marking the old behavior as obsolete, since users will start writing code that depends on the new warnings.
- The underlying storage of the callback is unchanged, so the only thing being adjusted is what the compiler is told about the value this is worth calling out so the change is not mistaken for a deeper runtime change.

## Considered alternatives

- **Keep the current permissive behavior:** rejected — it keeps the same problem the original issue reported and gives callers weaker warnings than the rest of the C# type system.
- **Leave it to application developers to enforce nullability in their handlers:** rejected — every consumer would have to do the same work, with no compiler help; the framework should be the one to encode the rule.
- **Add a new overload that allows null even for non-nullable `TValue`:** rejected — it adds a second way to do the same thing and most users would just pick the looser one, leaving the original problem in place.
- **Chosen — tighten the annotations** so the compiler reports match the declared nullability of `TValue`. This is the approach proposed in the original issue.

## Open questions

- **Should the same fix also apply to related APIs?** For example, the `EventCallback.Factory.Create<TValue>` helpers, the `EventCallback<TValue>` constructor, and `IComponent.DispatchExceptionAsync`.
- **Will there be a deprecation notice?** Is the new strict behavior introduced immediately, or is the old shape marked `[Obsolete]` first to give users a warning period.
- **What is the migration plan inside the framework itself?** Some first-party code (such as `InputBase.OnChange`, `EditContext` notifications, and `NavigationManager.NavigateTo`) currently passes values that may be null, so those call sites need to be updated at the same time.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design for Enhance nullability support for TValue in EventCallback" (#67841)

Issues.Title: Enforce MultipartHeadersLengthLimit across BufferedReadStream buffers
Issues.Body:
- [x] You've read the [Contributor Guide](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CODE-OF-CONDUCT.md).
- [x] You've included unit or integration tests for your change, where applicable.
- [x] You've included inline docs for your change, where applicable.
- [x] There's an open issue for the PR that you are making.

Enforce `FormOptions.MultipartHeadersLengthLimit` across buffered reads

## Description

`BufferedReadStream.ProcessLineChar` compared a per-invocation `writeCount` against the line length limit while appending each buffer fragment to a shared `MemoryStream`. `ProcessLineChar` processes at most one buffer per call and resets `writeCount` to `0` on each call, so when a single multipart header line spans more than one buffer (default buffer is 4 KiB, default header-line limit is 16 KiB) the per-buffer count always stayed below the limit and the check never fired. The line kept accumulating in the backing `MemoryStream` across invocations, so the documented 16,384-byte `MultipartHeadersLengthLimit` was not enforced for header lines that span multiple buffers.

This restores the pre-#51426 behavior by comparing the cumulative accumulated length (`builder.Length + writeCount`) against the limit. `builder.Length` is the number of bytes flushed by previous invocations, and `writeCount` is the number of bytes consumed from the current buffer that have not been flushed yet, so their sum is the total line length seen so far.

Added tests:
- `BufferedReadStreamTests` covering `ReadLine`/`ReadLineAsync` for lines that span multiple buffers (within and over the limit) and an unterminated over-limit line.
- `MultipartReaderTests.MultipartReader_HeaderLineSpanningMultipleBuffers_EnforcesHeadersLengthLimit` covering the end-to-end form-parsing path.

All were confirmed to fail before the fix and pass after. Full `Microsoft.AspNetCore.WebUtilities.Tests` suite passes (488 passed, 2 skipped).

Fixes #67838
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Enforce MultipartHeadersLengthLimit across BufferedReadStream buffers" (#67840)

Issues.Title: `FormOptions.MultipartHeadersLengthLimit` not enforced when a multipart header line spans multiple `BufferedReadStream` buffers
Issues.Body:
The 16,384-byte `MultipartHeadersLengthLimit` is not enforced when a single multipart header line spans more than one internal `BufferedReadStream` buffer. `ProcessLineChar` resets its `writeCount` on every buffer-processing invocation and compares that per-buffer count against the total line limit, while still appending each fragment to the same backing `MemoryStream`. Since the default buffer (4 KiB) is smaller than the default limit (16 KiB), the check never fires and an unterminated header line keeps growing in memory well past 16 KiB.

### Affected versions
- Reproduces: 9.0.17, 10.0.9
- Not affected: 8.0.x (retains the earlier cumulative check)

## Root cause
Introduced by #51426. The cumulative check `builder.Length > lengthLimit` was replaced with a per-invocation `writeCount > lengthLimit` inside `ProcessLineChar`, where `writeCount` is reset to `0` each call and only ever counts one buffer's worth of bytes (≤ 4096 < 16384), so it never trips.

### Expected vs. actual
- Expected: a header line over the limit is rejected promptly with `InvalidDataException`,
  regardless of how it splits across buffers.
- Actual: the line is accepted and accumulated as long as it stays unterminated.

### Suggested fix
Compare the limit against the cumulative accumulated length instead of the per-invocation `writeCount`. Add a regression test with a header line spanning ≥ 2 buffers that exceeds the header limit.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "`FormOptions.MultipartHeadersLengthLimit` not enforced when a multipart header line spans multiple `BufferedReadStream` buffers" (#67838)

Issues.Title: Design Proposal: Make ColumnBase.IsSortableByDefault() Public in QuickGrid
Issues.Body: # Design Proposal: Make `ColumnBase.IsSortableByDefault()` Public in QuickGrid ## Summary QuickGrid's `ColumnBase` exposes a `HeaderTemplate` that lets consumers render a custom header, but the default header relies on the `IsSortableByDefault()` helper, which was declared `protected`. Because that helper was not reachable from user code, consumers authoring custom headers could not determine whether a column is sortable by default. This feature changes the access modifier of `IsSortableByDefault()` from `protected virtual` to `public virtual` on `ColumnBase`, enabling external callers to query the default sortability of a column. [#44898](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/44898) ## Motivation and Goals - Allow developers who supply a custom `HeaderTemplate` to correctly determine whether the column participates in sorting by default. - Unblock scenarios such as custom export utilities that need to know which columns are sortable when iterating the grid's columns. - Keep the change minimal and surgical, exposing an existing, well-defined virtual method rather than introducing a new surface. - Preserve the virtual contract so derived column types can continue to override the default sortability decision. ## In Scope - Changing the access modifier of `IsSortableByDefault()` on `ColumnBase` in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid` from `protected virtual` to `public virtual`. - Retaining the existing `virtual` semantics so derived columns may continue to override the method. - Adding unit tests that verify the method is callable from outside the type hierarchy and returns the expected default value. - Updating the associated public API baseline files to reflect the new accessibility of the member. ## Out of Scope - Changing the signature, return type, or behavior of `IsSortableByDefault()`. - Adding new public API surface beyond the existing member (no new properties, methods, or types). - Modifying `PropertyColumn`, `TemplateColumn`, or any other derived column's default sort logic. - Changes to the default header rendering performed by `ColumnBase.RenderDefaultHeaderContent(RenderTreeBuilder)`. ## Risks and Unknowns - Public surface increase: the method becomes part of the supported public contract and must remain source- and binary-compatible going forward. - Subclassers that previously relied on `protected` access are unaffected, but any future redesign of the method must account for external callers now that it is `public`. - Binary-compat tooling (e.g., API baseline checks) must be updated; otherwise, the build can fail until the baseline is refreshed. - No behavioral risk is introduced because the method's implementation and override semantics are unchanged. - Long-term behavioral contract: once `public`, any future change to the default-sort logic (e.g., factoring in user permissions or a new column option) becomes a behavioral breaking change rather than an internal refactor. - Subclasser CS0108 warning risk: existing subclasses that already declare a same-signature `protected virtual bool IsSortableByDefault()` override will see the C# compiler emit CS0108 ("hides inherited member; add `new` keyword if hiding was intended") because the base accessor is now `public virtual`. This is a source-level side effect that must be called out in migration guidance. ## API Surface ```diff public abstract partial class ColumnBase { - protected virtual bool IsSortableByDefault(); + public virtual bool IsSortableByDefault(); } ``` - The return type (`bool`), name, parameters, and `virtual` modifier are unchanged. - Namespace: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid`. ## Edge Case Scenarios - A custom `HeaderTemplate` author calls `IsSortableByDefault()` directly on a `ColumnBase` reference, including references typed as the base class rather than the concrete derived column type. - A derived column type overrides `IsSortableByDefault()`; the overridden implementation is reachable through the now-public method, and behavior is preserved. - A non-sortable column (e.g., a `TemplateColumn` that opts out of sorting) returns `false` from the public call, allowing export or rendering logic to skip it. - A sortable column with an explicitly assigned `SortBy` expression returns `true`, matching the behavior used by the built-in default header. ## Examples ```csharp // Inside a custom HeaderTemplate or any consumer of QuickGrid: foreach (var column in grid.Columns) { if (column.IsSortableByDefault()) { // Emit sortable header affordance or include in sortable export. } } ``` ```razor @* Using IsSortableByDefault() inside a custom HeaderTemplate *@ @if (context.IsSortableByDefault()) { @context.Title } else { @context.Title } ``` ## Alternative Designs - **Read-only property (`bool IsSortableByDefault`)** — Rejected to avoid introducing a new member and to preserve the existing `virtual` method contract used by derived columns. - **New public wrapper (e.g., `IsSortable()`)** — Rejected because it duplicates an existing virtual and increases the API surface unnecessarily. - **Document a consumer-side workaround** — Rejected because the default sortability logic is an implementation detail and should remain the framework's responsibility. - **Move the method to a public interface (e.g., `ISortableColumn`)** — Rejected because it would add new public surface, force every existing column subclass to implement the interface, and break the existing override contract that derived columns rely on today. ## Breaking Changes None. The change only widens accessibility; it does not alter signatures, return types, or runtime behavior. Existing subclasses and consumers continue to compile and behave identically. **Migration / subclasser effects:** Consumers who have already declared a same-signature `protected virtual bool IsSortableByDefault()` override in a derived `ColumnBase` subclass will receive a CS0108 compiler warning, because the new `public virtual` base member hides the subclass's `protected virtual` declaration. The recommended mitigation is to either (a) widen the subclass override to `public virtual` for explicit consistency, or (b) add the `new` keyword if the hiding behavior is intentional. ## Testing Strategy - Add a unit test asserting that `IsSortableByDefault()` is callable from outside the `ColumnBase` hierarchy and returns the expected default value for a representative column. - Add a unit test that constructs a derived column overriding `IsSortableByDefault()` and verifies the override is invoked through the public accessor. - Run the existing QuickGrid test suite to confirm no regressions in default header rendering, sorting, or column enumeration. - Add a baseline-fixture test that snapshots the API surface diff to lock down the new `public` accessibility of the member. ## Drawbacks - Increases the public API surface by one member, which adds permanent maintenance and documentation burden. - Once `public`, the method's semantics become a contract — any future change to the default-sort logic (e.g., factoring in user permissions or a new column option) is effectively a behavioral breaking change rather than an internal refactor. - Subclasser side effect: existing subclasses with a same-signature `protected virtual` override will see CS0108 warnings after this change is consumed, creating migration noise for downstream users. - The accessor change cannot be reverted without a major-version break, so the decision is effectively one-way.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design Proposal: Make ColumnBase.IsSortableByDefault() Public in QuickGrid" (#67837)

Issues.Title: Design for preserve null values for in single select binding
Issues.Body:
## Preserve null values for in single select binding

Allows `<option value="@null">` to be bound back as a C# `null` value in Blazor `InputSelect`  scenarios by emitting a framework-internal marker attribute on null-designated options, eliminating the need for application-level empty-string-to-null conversion. #23934

## Motivation and goals

- Eliminate the need for application code that manually converts empty-string selections into null values.
- Support scenarios where a developer writes an option intended to represent a null value, such as a placeholder selection.
- Preserve normal HTML rendering while allowing Blazor binding logic to recognize a null selection.
- Improve developer experience for InputSelect binding involving nullable values.

## In scope

- Add a marker attribute for options originating from Razor null values.
- Detect the marker during select binding.
- Return null from binding and change-event processing when the selected option represents a null value.
- Preserve existing empty-string behavior for options that are not marked as null.

## Out of scope

- Supporting simultaneous distinction between both an actual empty string ("") option and a null option within the same HTML select element. The issue explicitly notes HTML limitations in this area.
- Changes to non-select form controls.
- Changes to general HTML behavior outside Blazor binding.
- New form-validation features unrelated to select null handling.

## Risks / unknowns

- HTML does not inherently differentiate null from an empty string in option values, requiring framework-specific handling.
- Existing applications that intentionally relied on empty-string-to-null conversion semantics may need to understand the new null-option behavior.
- Behavior remains constrained by browser select-element semantics.
- The solution depends on framework-generated marker metadata because HTML does not natively distinguish null from an empty string in <option> values.
- Correct behavior requires the marker information to remain synchronized between rendering, binding, and change-event processing; if any of these stages diverges, the behavior silently regresses to the legacy empty-string semantics.
- **SSR / prerendering consistency:** the marker attribute must be emitted identically in both interactive and prerendered output so that hydration does not observe a mismatched attribute set and the marker survives the SSR → client transition.
- **EditForm and traditional form-post:** the marker is a Blazor binding concept; traditional form-post serialization (non-Blazor submission) will still observe an empty string, so the developer-experience win is Blazor-specific.
- **Backward compatibility:** CSS selectors and JS interop that target the affected `<option>` elements may need review, and any consumer-side behavior that assumed a single canonical empty-string option value may need to be revisited.

## Edge Case Scenarios

- Selecting a marked null option results in a bound value of null.
- Selecting an unmarked empty-string option continues to produce an empty string.
- Switching between a concrete value and a marked null option correctly transitions between the concrete value and null.
- Multiple options that render with empty-string values remain distinguishable only through the framework's marker attribute.

## Examples

```razor
    <InputSelect @bind-Value="Model.CategoryId">
        <option value="@null">Select one...</option>
        <option value="1">Category 1</option>
        <option value="2">Category 2</option>
    </InputSelect>
```

## Detailed design

- **Marker attribute:** rendering emits a framework-internal attribute, `blazor-option-value-is-null` (placeholder name; final name is a framework implementation detail and is **not** a documented public API), on any `<option>` whose Razor-authored value is `null`. The attribute is only present for null-valued options and is omitted for all other values, including those that happen to evaluate to `null` at runtime but were not authored with a literal `@null`.
- **Emission point:** the marker is added by the option-rendering helper used by both `InputSelect` and native `<select>` rendering paths, so it is independent of any specific input component.
- **Binding detection:** during binding, the change-event handler inspects the selected option for the marker attribute. Detection lives in the shared binding handler pipeline and therefore applies to both `InputSelect` and native `<select>` consumers.
- **Marker present → `null`:** the bound value is produced as `null` and the change-event pipeline preserves it as `null`.
- **Marker absent → legacy behavior:** the existing empty-string semantics are preserved for options that are not marked.
- **HTML limitation:** both `null` and empty-string option values are represented using the same underlying select-element value; the marker attribute is the sole framework-level distinction.

## Drawbacks

- The framework-emitted marker attribute adds a small rendering cost and slightly increases HTML serialization size for every null option.
- Future framework changes that inadvertently remove or rename the marker would silently break consumers relying on it via CSS selectors, JS interop, or test assertions; any such change must be treated as a behavioral breaking change.
- The `null` round-trip works through Blazor's binding pipeline, but **not** through traditional form-post serialization, which still observes an empty string. The developer-experience win is Blazor-specific.
- The rendered DOM no longer perfectly mirrors what an equivalent non-Blazor Razor app would produce, which can confuse developers inspecting the page or making manual HTTP requests.
- Tests that snapshot rendered HTML for assertions will need to be updated when null options are present.

## Considered alternatives

- **Status quo — continue treating `""` and `null` as equivalent and require manual conversion in application code.** This is the pre-existing behavior established by the predecessor change (PR #23221) and reflected in the original issue. The user pain is real: every consumer of `InputSelect` with a nullable bound value has to write `string.IsNullOrEmpty(x) ? null : x` and back, which is noisy, error-prone, and inconsistent with how nullable types are otherwise handled in C#.
- **Rely solely on HTML option values.** Rejected because HTML cannot distinguish `null` from an empty string inside a single `<select>`; no amount of consumer-side logic can recover the lost information once the option value has been serialized to the DOM.
- **Consumer-data-attribute convention** (e.g., require authors to add `data-blazor-null="true"` themselves). Rejected because it places the burden on every application author and reintroduces a manual step that the framework can perform reliably and consistently on the author's behalf.
- **Chosen — introduce framework-level identification of null-designated options so binding can restore the intended C# `null` value.** This is the approach proposed in the original issue and reflected by the implemented enhancement. The framework emits the marker, the binding pipeline reads it, and the round-trip works without any application code.

## Open questions

- Is the marker attribute name finalized, and is it confirmed to be private to framework internals (i.e., not surfaced in public documentation)?
- Should the marker also be emitted for `<option selected>` initial-render scenarios, where the bound model is `null` but no user interaction has occurred yet?
- Does the marker preservation need to survive across prerendering / SSR hydration, and if so, how is the attribute serialized to the initial HTML payload?
- Should `EditForm` model binding benefit from the same marker, or is the scope strictly limited to `@bind-Value` / `@bind`?
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design for preserve null values for in single select binding" (#67835)

Issues.Title: Design Proposal: Dynamic Columns for QuickGrid
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Enable reusable, runtime-driven column definitions for `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` by adding a `DynamicColumn<TGridItem>` description type and a `Columns` parameter. Columns defined in C# can be shared across multiple grids, loaded from configuration, and toggled at runtime with `ShowColumnAsync` / `HideColumnAsync`.

## Motivation and goals

- [Blazor QuickGrid Dynamic Columns (#47218)](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/47218) highlights the need to define and manage QuickGrid columns dynamically instead of relying only on static Razor child content.
- Columns declared directly in ChildContent are difficult to reuse across multiple grids, which leads to repeated markup and duplicated column logic.
- Runtime-driven column scenarios, such as columns loaded from configuration, APIs, user preferences, or a shared column manager, currently require custom workarounds.
- Optional columns are usually handled with conditional Razor blocks, which makes the grid markup harder to maintain as the column set grows.
- The goal is to make dynamic columns reusable, configurable, and controllable at runtime while preserving existing QuickGrid behavior such as sorting, virtualization, column options, and existing static column support.

## In scope

- New `DynamicColumn<TGridItem>` public type supporting `Property` (expression) or `Template` (RenderFragment).
- New `Columns` parameter on `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` to accept an enumerable of dynamic column definitions.
- `Visible` flag on each column; `ShowColumnAsync(columnKey)` / `HideColumnAsync(columnKey)` methods to toggle at runtime.
- Materialization of each dynamic column into the appropriate `PropertyColumn<TGridItem,TProp>` or `TemplateColumn<TGridItem>`.
- Column ordering rule: dynamic columns appear before `ChildContent` columns.
- Optional `Key` property for unambiguous column lookup (falls back to case-insensitive `Title` matching).
- Integration with existing sort, paging, virtualization, and ARIA features without new code paths.

## Out of scope

- Column reordering or drag-and-drop rearrangement.
- Persistence of visibility state across page reloads.
- Per-column UI customization beyond what `ColumnOptions` already provides.
- Removing or replacing the existing `ChildContent` API; `Columns` is purely additive.
- Changing how POST-based forms bind data (this is a QuickGrid feature only).

## Risks / unknowns

**Uniqueness and lookup:**
- `Title` is a display string, not guaranteed unique. Callers should use optional `Key` for unambiguous lookup, or ensure `Title` uniqueness within a grid.

**Performance and overhead:**
- Each render evaluates `Columns` in `OnParametersSet`. Large grids (100+ dynamic columns) may see measurable cost, but the pattern is already established by static `ChildContent` analysis.

**Virtualization compatibility:**
- Dynamic column visibility toggling while the grid is virtualized should work correctly (column collection re-runs on `OnParametersSet`), but edge cases need confirmation via testing.

**No runtime `Add` / `Remove`:**
- The proposal supports show/hide for pre-defined columns only. Adding a brand-new column at runtime is not supported because it would require interaction with the `Items` queryable (which may already be sorted/paged).

## Examples

**Example 1: Reuse columns across two grids**

```csharp
private IReadOnlyList<DynamicColumn<Person>> _columns =
[
    new() { Title = "ID", Property = p => p.PersonId, Visible = true },
    new() { Title = "Name", Property = p => p.LastName, Visible = true },
];

// Both grids share the same column definitions
<QuickGrid Items="@people" Columns="@_columns" />
<QuickGrid Items="@managers" Columns="@_columns" />
```

**Example 2: Runtime column visibility toggle**

```csharp
<button @onclick='() => grid!.ShowColumnAsync("Salary")'>Show salary</button>
<button @onclick='() => grid!.HideColumnAsync("Salary")'>Hide salary</button>

<QuickGrid @ref="grid" Items="@employees" Columns="@_columns" />
```

**Example 3: Load columns from configuration**

```csharp
private IReadOnlyList<DynamicColumn<Order>> _columns =
    _config.GetSection("GridColumns")
        .Get<ColumnConfig[]>()
        .Select(c => new DynamicColumn<Order>
        {
            Title = c.Title,
            Property = c.PropertyExpression,  // compiled or pre-built
            Visible = c.IsEnabledByDefault,
        })
        .ToList();
```

This third pattern is **impossible with the current `ChildContent` model** because Razor markup cannot be built at runtime.



## Detailed design

### Design principles

1. **Reuse existing components.** `DynamicColumn` is a *description* materialized into `PropertyColumn<TGridItem,TProp>` or `TemplateColumn<TGridItem>`, so sort, options UI, ARIA, and virtualization need no new code paths.
2. **Preserve QuickGrid behavior.** Existing surface (`ChildContent`, `Items`, sort, paging, virtualization, row click, `ColumnOptions`, query parameters) is unchanged.
3. **No breaking changes.** Purely additive; existing code unaffected.
4. **Mixed columns.** `Columns` and `ChildContent` coexist, as documented in [Column ordering](#column-ordering).
5. **Cheap to ignore.** No extra work for grids that don't set `Columns`.

### Implementation outline

- Introduce `DynamicColumn<TGridItem>` as a new public type inheriting `ColumnBase<TGridItem>`.
- Add a `Columns` parameter to `QuickGrid<TGridItem>`.
- Implement `RenderDynamicColumn` to iterate visible columns and materialize each into `PropertyColumn` or `TemplateColumn`.
- Add `ShowColumnAsync` and `HideColumnAsync` methods for runtime visibility control.
- Preserve the existing sequential stream behavior for callers that use `ChildContent` only.

### Internal behavior

- `RenderDynamicColumn` invokes between `StartCollectingColumns()` and `@ChildContent`, ensuring dynamic columns appear before static columns in the final list.
- For each visible `DynamicColumn`:
  - If `Template` is set, materialize `<TemplateColumn<TGridItem>>` wrapping the template.
- `Visible` is a plain auto-property (no `PropertyChanged` event); direct mutation is supported and triggers re-render via `StateHasChanged`.
- Column lookups in `ShowColumnAsync` / `HideColumnAsync` prefer `Key` (if any column has one) or fall back to case-insensitive `Title` matching.



## Considered alternatives

### A. Runtime Column Registration API (`AddColumn` / `RemoveColumn`)

**Rejected:** introduces mutable grid state outside Blazor's normal parameter-driven rendering model. It increases complexity around synchronization with sorting, pagination, virtualization, and component lifecycle events. The approach also makes grid behavior less predictable during re-renders and would require a significantly larger API surface.

### B. RenderFragment-Based Dynamic Column Generation

**Rejected:** while flexible, this pushes the responsibility of column materialization onto every consumer. Callers would need to construct `PropertyColumn` and `TemplateColumn` instances themselves, reducing type safety, increasing implementation complexity, and making future column-management features (visibility, persistence, customization) harder to standardize.

### C. Reflection-Based Auto-Generated Columns

**Rejected:** suitable only for simple property-backed scenarios. It cannot naturally support template-driven content such as buttons, badges, actions, or computed values. Additional metadata systems would be required for customization, resulting in a solution that is less flexible than the feature request requires.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design Proposal: Dynamic Columns for QuickGrid" (#67834)

Issues.Title: Design Proposal: Expose QuickGrid Columns to Derived Classes
Issues.Body:
# Design Proposal: Expose QuickGrid Columns to Derived Classes
## Summary

The `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` component stores its column list in a private `_columns` field, blocking developers who build derived grid components from enumerating columns for features such as CSV/Excel export. This change introduces a protected `Columns` property on `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` that returns the column list as `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`, letting derived classes access column metadata (including `Title`) without exposing the list publicly. [#45398](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/45398)

## Motivation and Goals

- The private `_columns` field prevents derived `QuickGrid` components from implementing features that need to enumerate columns, such as building an export header row from each column's `Title`.
- Provide a stable extensibility point for derived classes while keeping the list read-only for outside consumers.
- Support a realistic export scenario (CSV/Excel) by allowing a derived component to read `ColumnBase<TGridItem>.Title` for each defined column.
- Preserve existing QuickGrid behavior and public surface area for non-derived consumers.

## API Surface

- New protected member on `QuickGrid<TGridItem>`:

```diff
namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid;
public partial class QuickGrid<TGridItem>
{
+    protected IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>> Columns { get; }
}
```

## In Scope

- Add a new protected `Columns` property on `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` that exposes the existing `_columns` list as `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`.
- Expose the property to the public API baseline via `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` so derived assemblies can reference it.
- Add xUnit tests that verify a derived class can read `Columns`, that the type is `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`, and that `Title` values are accessible for an export-style enumeration.

## Out of Scope

- Exposing `Columns` as a fully public (non-protected) API.
- Filtering or projecting columns (for example, returning only `PropertyColumn` instances) — only the full column list is exposed.
- Adding new built-in export functionality (CSV/Excel) to QuickGrid itself.
- Changes to column rendering, sorting, paging, virtualization, or any other QuickGrid behavior.
- Public APIs to mutate the column list from outside the base class.

## Risks and Unknowns

### Behavioral / API risks

- **Order coupling** — Derived classes could become coupled to the order and content of `Columns`; callers must tolerate the same list ordering the grid already uses during rendering.
- **Reference lifetime** — The exposed list is a read-only wrapper over an internal `List<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`, so a derived class cannot safely cache the reference across renders if the grid mutates the underlying list.
- **No `PropertyColumn`-only enumerator** — The implementation does not provide a public enumerator over only `PropertyColumn` instances, so derived classes that want to skip `TemplateColumn` entries for export must filter themselves (as the issue author suggested as an alternative).
- **Backward compatibility with reflection-based accessors** — Existing derived classes that currently reach into `_columns` via reflection (an undocumented pattern) will continue to work because the private field is not being removed or renamed. The new `Columns` property is purely additive and does not deprecate any prior surface.
- **Subclass evolution risk** — Once `Columns` is part of the protected contract, future changes to its element type (for example, rebinding to `IReadOnlyList<IComponent>` or to a deferred `IAsyncEnumerable<…>`) effectively become source-breaking changes for any consumer that has already subclassed `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` and referenced `Columns`.

### Edge Case Scenarios

- A derived component accesses `Columns` before the grid has rendered — the list is empty, matching the prior private behavior; callers must handle zero columns.
- A grid defines both `PropertyColumn` and `TemplateColumn` instances — `Columns` returns both; derived export code must filter to `PropertyColumn` if `TemplateColumn` headers should be excluded.
- A derived component reads `Columns` after the grid has collected its child column components during render — the list reflects the order in which columns were declared.
- A derived component calls `Columns` from outside an active render, then the grid re-renders with additional columns — the previously obtained reference still resolves through the same underlying list and may reflect a new count.

## Examples

### Example 1 — Build a CSV header from all columns

```csharp
public class ExportableGrid<TGridItem> : QuickGrid<TGridItem>
{
    public string BuildCsvHeader()
    {
        // Enumerate the protected Columns list and join each column's Title.
        return string.Join(",", Columns.Select(c => c.Title));
    }
}
```

### Example 2 — Filter to `PropertyColumn` only

```csharp
public class PropertyOnlyExportableGrid<TGridItem> : QuickGrid<TGridItem>
{
    public IEnumerable<string> ExportableHeaders()
        => Columns
            .OfType<PropertyColumn<TGridItem, object>>()
            .Select(c => c.Title);
}
```

## Detailed Design

- **Field-to-property bridging** — The existing private field `_columns` (of type `List<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>` in `QuickGrid.razor.cs`) is the canonical storage; the new `Columns` property returns it directly cast/exposed as `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`. No duplicate list, no copy on access, and no per-render allocation.
- **Initialization point** — The property has no explicit initialization step. It becomes non-empty as soon as the existing child-content collection logic appends columns to `_columns` during the grid's render (the same lifecycle that powers column rendering today).
- **Shape mapping for `IReadOnlyList<>`** — The exposed type gives derived classes `Count`, indexed access (`Columns[i]`), and enumeration. This is the minimum surface needed to build a header row, and it matches the issue author's preferred return type.
- **Thread-safety** — Derived classes must call `Columns` on the Blazor synchronization context. Cross-thread access (for example, from `Task.Run` or a `ThreadPool` continuation) is not part of the contract; concurrent mutation during a re-render can otherwise surface a torn view.
- **Downgrade semantics** — Because the property returns the live `_columns` instance, a snapshot taken before a re-render is not safe to retain across renders; callers needing stability must materialize their own list (`Columns.ToList()`).
- **No `InternalsVisibleTo` change required for the property itself** — The property is `protected`, so any subclass can see it directly. The test project still relies on `InternalsVisibleTo` for accessing the private `_columns` field to seed test scenarios.

## Drawbacks

- **Forever-maintained contract** — Every addition to the protected surface becomes a long-term compatibility constraint. Renaming, retyping, or removing `Columns` will be a source-breaking change for any subclasser that has taken a dependency on it.
- **Silent order sensitivity** — The order of `Columns` is tied to Razor declaration order in the consumer's markup. If a consumer reorders child content, derived classes that index into `Columns` (for example, `Columns[0]`) will quietly produce different output.
- **Cross-thread hazard** — A derived class that calls `Columns` from a non-Blazor synchronization context may observe a count or order inconsistent with the most recently rendered view.
- **Reduced refactoring freedom** — Adding to the protected surface reduces the team's ability to re-architect grid internals freely in future majors; future renames or rebindings of `Columns` become source-breaking changes for subclassers.
- **Reflection-based callers left in the cold** — The new property is C#-only; reflection-based code that previously probed `_columns` continues to work but does not benefit from a documented, stable surface.

## Considered Alternatives

- **Make `_columns` itself `protected` and expose the mutable `List<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>` directly** — *Rejected* because it would let derived classes mutate the internal list and break rendering invariants (reordering, double-render, missing-column crashes).
- **Expose a public method that returns the column list** — *Rejected* in favor of the protected property to keep the list inaccessible to non-derived consumers, matching the issue author's preferred option. A public method would advertise a general-purpose API that the team is not prepared to commit to.
- **Expose a separate read-only list filtered to `PropertyColumn` only** — *Rejected* because the issue author was open to the broader list; the implementation keeps `Columns` generic over `ColumnBase<TGridItem>` and leaves filtering to derived code (see Example 2).
- **Add a `protected virtual` hook method (for example, `protected virtual IEnumerable<string> GetColumnTitles()`)** — *Rejected* because it forces every derived class into the same enumeration strategy and forecloses non-title-based scenarios the issue author might want in the future. A property is more flexible than a hook.

## Breaking Changes

- **None for existing public API consumers.** The change only adds a new protected member; the existing private `_columns` field and all public/protected members are unchanged, so existing consumers (including derived classes that do not reference `Columns`) are unaffected.

## Testing Strategy

- Add a new test class `ColumnsPropertyTest` in the QuickGrid test project that derives a `QuickGridWithAccessibleColumns<TGridItem>` from `QuickGrid<TGridItem>` to surface the protected `Columns` property via an internal helper.
- Verify `Columns` is empty before any column collection occurs.
- Verify the runtime type returned from `Columns` is assignable to `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`.
- **Assert the property's declared return type is exactly `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`** (not `IList`, not `List`, not `IEnumerable`), to lock in the contract.
- Verify the protected property is reachable from a derived class and returns a non-null `IReadOnlyList<ColumnBase<TGridItem>>`.
- Populate the internal `_columns` list (simulating the render-time collection) and assert that the exposed `Columns` list reports the same count, preserves declaration order, and exposes the expected `Title` values for `PropertyColumn` instances.
- Assert that a derived component can build a CSV header row by enumerating `Columns` and joining `Title` values (the export scenario from the issue).
- **Assert that filtering `Columns` with `OfType<PropertyColumn<TGridItem, object>>()` yields the expected subset**, demonstrating the documented consumer pattern for skipping `TemplateColumn` headers.
- Assert that `Title` is readable from both the concrete `PropertyColumn<TGridItem, TProp>` and its `ColumnBase<TGridItem>` base reference.
- The test project uses `InternalsVisibleTo` (the standard pattern for QuickGrid) to reach the private `_columns` field for seeding test scenarios; no new `InternalsVisibleTo` entry is required for the property itself.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design Proposal: Expose QuickGrid Columns to Derived Classes" (#67833)

Issues.Title: The non-scaffolded razor page "Register" does not recognise Password.RequiredLength.
Issues.Body:
### Describe the bug
Setting password length through 
```
public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services){
  ...
  services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
  {
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
  });
  ...
```
does not change the non-scaffolded razor page as it always says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long." if inputed password is < 6 characters.

### To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a new ASP.NET Core Web 2.1 application
2. Select "Web application (Model-View Controller)" 
3. Select "Change authentication"
3.1. Select "Individual User Account"
4. Select Ok to have the project scaffolded.
5. Open file Startup.cs
6. Add 
```
services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
{
  options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
});
```
to method `ConfigureServices`.
7. Press F5 (or select your favourite start-debug method).
8. In the web browser that pops up
8.1. Select Register
8.2. Type "a@example.com" as user name (or select your favourite fast-to-type email addresss).
8.3. Type "a" as password (or any password shorter than 6 characters).
8.4. Select Register

### Expected behavior
The web page should have been updated with information about successful creation of user.

### Actual behaviour
The web page says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long."

### Additional context
I have a question at stack overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53620708/how-do-i-set-password-options-in-aspnet-core-2-1 that explains the same issue if this is not clear enough.

This issue might be duplicate of #774.

Issues.Title: Remember me isPersistent understanding
Issues.Body:
This is more of a question but its not enough of a question that i could post it up on stack overflow without risking it just getting closed as primary opinion based or to broad.   So i am going to put it here in hopes of getting an answer.

The issue is the understanding of how remember me is supposed to work.    My problem is that its not doing what we would expect it to do.   

Management expects when a user logs in and clicks remember me that they will then not be required to type their login and password again for a month.  Whether or not they will still have to type in the 2fa code is up for debate.

What is actually happening is that i can close the browser reboot and come back for about an hour and 15 minutes and then i will be forced to type my password again.    This still happens even with a valid `.AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie set that is not due to expire for another month.

After digging around in the code i found that Remember me is actually isPersistent and it is defined as.

>Flag indicating whether the sign-in cookie should persist after the browser is closed.

Which brings you to:

There are two different types of Cookies:

- session cookies, held in memory, and which expire once the browser exits
- persistent cookies, which have a time-to-live, are persisted on disk, and are sent by the browser until their time-to-live has elapsed.

All this being said remember machine 2fa works as expected i am not prompted again for the 2fa and i have a cookie on my machine `Identity.TwoFactorRememberMe`.

So I guess my question is why are we not getting a `Identity.FactorRememberMe` or why isn't sign in respecting a long life time of the `AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie?

How is this supposed to work exactly?   I am following the code here even though we have normal MVC and not razor pages like you have in the example.  I am still just calling 

>var signInUserResult = await _signInManager.PasswordSignInAsync(userName, password, rememberMe, true);

Note: I am using Asp .net Identity in Identity server 4 application.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Remember me isPersistent understanding" (#2079)

Issues.Title: Login doesn't show registered social logins after error
Issues.Body:
I'm using ASPNET Core 2.1 with Razor Pages UI Library and noticed that whilst registered social logins appear when I open the Login page, they disappear after an error like incorrect password.

There seems to be a bug in Identity\Pages\Account\Login.cshtml.cs as 

Please confirm this is a bug and comment on my fix

Issues.Title: Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?
Issues.Body:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.identity.entityframeworkcore.identityuser?view=aspnetcore-1.1

I'm looking through various implementations from the [readme](https://github.com/aspnet/Identity/blob/master/README.md) and I've noticed they all use `IdentityUser ` for their concrete type to pass to `UserManager`, or have had to craft a custom type to be able to have a contract between the database and the .NET AspNetIdentity implentation they've written (see the DynamoDB library).

I've had to do this too for the [Marten implementation I've written](https://github.com/yetanotherchris/Marten.AspNetIdentity), and the reason is, a `TUser` that your `UserStore` takes, should really be an interface - it will always have an email, most likely an Id of some sort, firstname, lastname and then a bag of properties.

Things become even more abstract when dealing with claims.

If everyone is using `IdentityUser` or their own custom `TUser` doesn't this defeat the purpose of AspnetIdentity - which is to be able to switch and change implementations easily? That isn't possible if your consumer is tightly coupled with a `TUser` implementation of the library.

### Example `TUser` implementations

- [MongoIdentityUser](https://github.com/tugberkugurlu/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/MongoIdentityUser.cs)
- [DynamoIdentityUser](https://github.com/miltador/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/DynamoIdentityUser.cs)
- [Redis uses `IdentityUser<string>`](https://github.com/aguacongas/Identity.Redis/blob/develop/src/Aguacongas.Identity.Redis/UserStore.cs)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?" (#2056)

Issues.Title: [Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?
Issues.Body:
My security team escalated to me that the `PasswordHasher` is vulnerable to heap inspection, as the password is of type `String`. 
It should be instead a byte array / span of bytes cleared at the end of the process, as it is done by any cryptographic operation in corefx. CC @bartonjs.
Same problem with any password related types (`IPasswordValidator`, and maybe also the `IUserPasswordStore`)

Issues.Title: Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019
Issues.Body:
Apologize if my feedback may sound controversial or may spark a debate.

I need to be sure that the problem mentioned on this [page](https://github.com/aspnet/Tooling/blob/master/known-issues-vs2017.md#to-create-aspnet-core-10--11-projects-install-visual-studios-net-core-10---11-developer-tools-for-web-component) about .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling is installed when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project should not happen or should not be supported in VS 2019.

These are my main concerns:

1. VS 2019 is about to be released in around early 2019
2. ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 is not going to be supported anymore, based on [this page](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/microsoft-support.md).
3. Creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 will always install the .NET Core 1.0/1.1 web tooling, and this is not necessary when previous reason has reached the unsupported period and it's quite near or might be the same time of VS 2019 release.
4. Based on the problem mentioned above, VS 2017 has no "built in" tooling when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project, but the external tooling may help. Therefore for those that still need to maintain ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 projects, we could use VS 2017 as long as the VS 2017 is still in support lifecycle or in mainstream support lifecycle.

I welcome feedback 🙂
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019" (#1078)

Issues.Title: Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json
Issues.Body:
I used to be able to use `dotnet watch` in Visual Studio by adding the following section to `launchSettings.json` and starting the project without debugging using CTRL + F5.

```
    "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    },
```

This used to work in ASP.NET Core 1.1 but no longer does. According to https://github.com/aspnet/DotNetTools/issues/404 the new format for ASP.NET Core 2.0 is:

```
   "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "workingDirectory": "C:\\Users\\namc\\source\\repos\\WebApplication2\\WebApplication2",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    }
```

The problem with this is the hard coded `executablePath` which is Windows specific and the `workingDirectory` which is specific to wherever I did a Git clone on my local machine.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json" (#1067)

Issues.Title: Nesting bug
Issues.Body:
The is a problem nesting embedded resources.  I was experimenting with nesting .cshtml files using the existing rules and did this:

Modal.cs (bootstrap modal web component)
   -> Modal.cs.cshtml (it's view file)

`Modal.cs.cshtml` is set to Embedded Resource, and clearly shows it, but is completed IGNORED by the build process (simply open the DLL in a text editor [such as Notepad++] and scroll to the bottom and look for text).  If I rename the file to "Modal.cshtml" instead it works perfectly fine!  :/  I don't think whether or not a file is embedded should have and role in excluding a file from being embedded so this seems like a bug to me.

Yes, I'm aware of upcoming nesting controls in v15.6, but I'm not able to install it yet, and regardless, this should work fine, so it should be looked into nonetheless.

Just to add: I'm using wild-cards to include the file.  If I explicitly add it I get this:

`Duplicate 'EmbeddedResource' items were included. ...`

So it's clearly found.  It's obviously something up with the build process not finding the nested file.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Nesting bug" (#1066)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: `OpenApiVersion` default change from `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1` to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`
Issues.Body:
### Description

The default OpenApi spec version is changing to 3.2 instead of 3.1.

### Version

.NET 11 Preview 6

### Previous behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaulted to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1`

### New behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaults to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [ ] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We aim to always use the latest version of the spec by default.

### Recommended action

Generally, no action is needed except if users encounter problems, like other toolings that don't support OpenApi 3.2.

If you're impacted, you can always configure the OpenApiOptions to use 3.1:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenApi(options =>
{
    options.OpenApiVersion = OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1;
});
```

### Affected APIs

- `OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: `OpenApiVersion` default change from `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1` to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`" (#530)

Issues.Title: ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027
Issues.Body:
# ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027

> Update: The ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support date was updated from April 7, 2027 to April 13, 2027 to align with the Microsoft servicing release cycles.

Today we are announcing that ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach **end of support on April 13, 2027**. After that date, Microsoft will no longer provide security patches, bug fixes, or technical support for ASP.NET Core 2.3.

This announcement provides the required 12 months of advance notice, as defined by the [Microsoft Support Lifecycle Policy](https://learn.microsoft.com/lifecycle/faq/general-lifecycle#what-is-the-lifecycle-policy-for-tools-) for products classified as "Tools."

## Current support policy

ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages (latest patched version only) are currently [supported](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet#dotnet-core) on .NET Framework, following the support cycle for those .NET Framework versions. After April 13, 2027, this support will end regardless of the .NET Framework version in use.

Note that the [ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/support/policy/aspnet/2.3-packages) include the Entity Framework 2.3 packages, which will end support on the same date.

## Impact of end of support

After ASP.NET Core 2.3 reaches end of support:

- No new security updates will be issued for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- Continuing to use an unsupported version may expose your applications to security vulnerabilities.
- Technical support will no longer be available for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- The ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages will be deprecated.

## Upgrade recommendation

We recommend upgrading to a [currently supported version of .NET](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet), such as .NET 10 (LTS).

To help with the upgrade process, we recommend using [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview), which provides AI-powered assistance to help you analyze, plan, and execute your migration to a modern .NET version.

## Resources

- [ASP.NET Core Support Policy](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet)
- [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview)
- [Migrate from ASP.NET to ASP.NET Core](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/proper-to-2x/)
- [Blog post: ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet-core-2-3-end-of-support/)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027" (#529)

# Deprecation of Microsoft ASP.NET WebHooks

[Microsoft ASP.NET WebHooks](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/webhooks/) provided support for sending and receiving WebHooks in ASP.NET 4.x applications on .NET Framework 4.5. The project has been in maintenance mode for several years with no planned feature work.

All `Microsoft.AspNet.WebHooks` NuGet packages are now **deprecated**. These packages will no longer receive any updates, including security patches, and are not recommended for use.

Issues.Title: Add automation to create docs tracking issues for breaking changes
Issues.Body:
Automates creation of documentation tracking issues in `dotnet/AspNetCore.Docs` when breaking change issues are opened or labeled in this repository.

## Implementation

**New workflow:** `.github/workflows/create-docs-issue.yaml`

- Triggers on `issues.opened` and `issues.labeled` events with "Breaking change" label
- Creates issue in docs repo with:
  - Title: `[Breaking Change] <original title>`
  - Body: Link to source issue, full context
  - Labels: `breaking-change`, `aspnet/Announcements`
- Posts comment on source issue with link to docs issue

## Duplicate Prevention

Multi-layer detection to handle race conditions and partial failures:
- Checks for existing bot comments in source issue
- Searches docs repo by title before creating
- Concurrency control keyed on issue number

## Error Handling

Separate try-catch blocks for each operation:
- Comment listing failures → proceed with search
- Search failures → proceed with creation (fail open to avoid missing docs)
- Comment posting failures → don't fail workflow (docs issue already created)
- Permission errors → explicit message about `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` requirement

## Setup Required

Configure `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` repository secret with `repo` scope for cross-repository issue creation. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` but will fail with clear error message.

## Security

Explicit minimal permissions: `issues: write`, `contents: read`

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Deprecating WebHostBuilder,  IWebHost, and WebHost
Issues.Body:
### Description

`WebHostBuilder` was replaced by `HostBuilder` ([generic host](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder)) in 3.0 and we also added [`WebApplicationBuilder`](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) in 6.0.

We are now marking `WebHostBuilder` as obsolete to encourage users to move on to the alternatives which is where future investments will occur, and we're also marking `IWebHost` and `WebHost` as deprecated to clean up other parts of the now obsolete web host story.

### Version

.NET 10 RC 1

### Previous behavior

```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

### New behavior

Using `WebHostBuilder` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR004**:
> warning ASPDEPR004: WebHostBuilder is deprecated in favor of HostBuilder and WebApplicationBuilder. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/004.

Using `IWebHost` or `WebHost` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR008**:
> warning ASPDEPR008: WebHost is obsolete. Use HostBuilder or WebApplicationBuilder instead. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/008.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We recommend the use of `HostBuilder` and `WebApplication` going forward over `WebHostBuilder` because they have all the features of `WebHostBuilder` and will be the focus of future investment.

### Recommended action

See https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/host/generic-host or https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/minimal-apis/webapplication for more details on the different hosting models. As well as [HostBuilder replaces WebHostBuilder](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder) and [Introducing WebApplication](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) for migration guides.

Below is an example of converting `WebHostBuilder` to `HostBuilder`:

**Before**
```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

**After**
```csharp
using var host = new HostBuilder()
    .ConfigureWebHost(webHostBuilder =>
    {
        webHostBuilder
            .UseTestServer() // If using TestServer
            .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
            .UseStartup()
            .UseKestrel();
    })
    .Build();
await host.StartAsync();

var testServer = host.GetTestServer();
```

Both Copilot and ChatGpt have given good results so far when asked to convert a WebHostBuilder example to use HostBuilder. Below is an example prompt:
<details>
<summary>Example AI Prompt</summary>

> Can you convert
>
> var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
>     .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
>     .UseStartup()
>     .UseKestrel();
> // Test code might use TestServer:
> var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
>
> to use HostBuilder.

</details>

### Affected APIs

`public class Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostBuilder`
`public interface Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.IWebHost`
`public static class Microsoft.AspNetCore.WebHost`

### Discussion

https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/63480

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Exception diagnostics are suppressed when `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns true
Issues.Body:
### Description

The [ASP.NET Core exception handler middleware](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/error-handling#exception-handler-page), `UseExceptionHandler()`, is responsible for catching and processing unhandled exceptions from ASP.NET Core requests.

In .NET 8, we introduced [`IExceptionHandler`](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/error-handling#iexceptionhandler). Implementations of `IExceptionHandler` are registered with dependency injection and invoked by the middleware. An implementation can return `true` from `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` to indicate that the exception has been handled. The middleware stops processing the exception once it has been handled.

In .NET 10, we're changing the default behavior so that the middleware no longer records diagnostics for exceptions handled by `IExceptionHandler`.

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

The exception handler middleware recorded diagnostics about exceptions handled by `IExceptionHandler`.

The exception diagnostics are:

- Logging `UnhandledException` to `ILogger`.
- Writing the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HandledException` event to `EventSource`.
- Adding the `error.type` tag to the `http.server.request.duration` metric.

### New behavior

If `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns `true`, then exception diagnostics are no longer recorded by default.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [ ] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

ASP.NET Core users have given feedback that the previous behavior was undesirable. Their `IExceptionHandler` implementation reported that the exception was handled, but the error handling middleware still recorded the error in the app's telemetry.

We are changing ASP.NET Core to follow the behavior expected by users by suppressing diagnostics when `IExceptionHandler` handles the exception. We're also adding configuration options to customize exception diagnostics behavior if needed.

### Recommended action

If you want handled exceptions to continue recording telemetry, you can use the new `ExceptionHandlerOptions.SuppressDiagnosticsCallback` option:

```cs
app.UseExceptionHandler(new ExceptionHandlerOptions
{
    SuppressDiagnosticsCallback = context => false;
});
```

The context passed to the callback includes information about the exception, the request, and whether the exception was handled. Returning `false` from the callback indicates that diagnostics shouldn't be suppressed. This restores the previous behavior.

### References

- Issue: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/54554
- PR: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/pull/59074

### Affected APIs

- `UseExceptionHandler()`
- `IExceptionHandler`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: Exception diagnostics are suppressed when `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns true" (#524)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete
Issues.Body:
### Description

Razor runtime compilation is obsolete and is not recommended for production scenarios. For production scenarios, use the default build time compilation. For development scenarios, use Hot Reload instead.

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

Developers could use [Razor runtime compilation](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/mvc/views/view-compilation) to recompile `.cshtml` files while the application is running. This is useful for development time so you don't need to restart the application for changes to take affect.

We're now obsoleting this and recommending developers make use of [Hot Reload](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/test/hot-reload) which has the same effect for development time.

### New behavior

Using the APIs listed below will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID `ASPDEPR003`:
> warning ASPDEPR003: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete and is not recommended for production scenarios. For production scenarios, use the default build time compilation. For development scenarios, use Hot Reload instead. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/003.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Razor Runtime compilation has been replaced by Hot Reload which has been the recommended approach for a few years now. We're making it clearer that Razor Runtime compilation is not getting support for new features and should no longer be used.

### Recommended action

Remove calls to `.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation()` and use Hot Reload.

### Affected APIs

`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.ApplicationParts.AssemblyPartExtensions`
`Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.RazorRuntimeCompilationMvcBuilderExtensions.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation`
`Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.RazorRuntimeCompilationMvcCoreBuilderExtensions.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation`
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Razor.RuntimeCompilation.FileProviderRazorProjectItem`
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Razor.RuntimeCompilation.MvcRazorRuntimeCompilationOptions`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete" (#522)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers property and MVC API analyzers are deprecated
Issues.Body:
## Description

The `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` MSBuild property and its associated MVC API analyzers are deprecated and will be removed in a future release. When `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is set to `true`, the build will now emit warning `ASPDEPR007`.

## Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

## Previous behavior

Previously, developers could set `<IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>` in their Web SDK projects to enable MVC API analyzers without any warnings or deprecation notices.

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net9.0</TargetFramework>
    <IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

The project would build successfully without any deprecation warnings.

## New behavior

Starting in .NET 10 Preview 7, when `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is set to `true`, the build will emit warning `ASPDEPR007`:

```
warning ASPDEPR007: The IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers property and its associated MVC API analyzers are deprecated and will be removed in a future release.
```

The analyzers will continue to function, but developers will receive this deprecation warning during compilation.

## Type of breaking change

- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

## Reason for change

## Reason for change

The MVC API analyzers were originally introduced to help keep return types and attributes in sync for API controllers, ensuring consistency between method signatures and their corresponding OpenAPI documentation. These analyzers provided compile-time validation to catch mismatches between declared return types and the actual types returned by controller actions.

However, with the introduction of Minimal APIs and the `TypedResults` pattern, the .NET ecosystem has evolved toward a more type-safe approach for API development. `TypedResults` provides compile-time guarantees about response types without requiring additional analyzers, making the MVC API analyzers redundant for modern .NET applications. In .NET 10, `TypedResults` are supported in controller-based APIs.

**Previous approach with MVC API analyzers:**
```csharp
[HttpGet]
[ProducesResponseType<Product>(200)]
[ProducesResponseType(404)]
public async Task<ActionResult> GetProduct(int id)
{
    var product = await _productService.GetByIdAsync(id);
    if (product == null)
        return NotFound(); // Analyzer ensures this matches ProducesResponseType(404)
    
    return Ok(product); // Analyzer ensures this matches ProducesResponseType<Product>(200)
}
```

**Or with controller actions using TypedResults:**
```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public async Task<Results<Ok<Product>, NotFound>> GetProduct(int id)
{
    var product = await _productService.GetByIdAsync(id);
    return product == null 
        ? TypedResults.NotFound() 
        : TypedResults.Ok(product);
}
```

The `TypedResults` pattern eliminates the need for separate analyzers because the return type itself (`Results<Ok<Product>, NotFound>`) explicitly declares all possible response types at compile time. This approach is more maintainable, provides better IntelliSense support, and automatically generates accurate OpenAPI documentation without additional tooling.

As the .NET ecosystem continues to embrace `TypedResults` as the recommended pattern for API development, the MVC API analyzers have become obsolete and are being deprecated to streamline the development experience.

## Recommended action

Developers should:

1. **Remove the deprecated property**: Remove `<IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>` from your project files to eliminate the warning.

2. **Suppress the warning temporarily**: If you need to continue using the deprecated analyzers temporarily, you can suppress the warning:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);ASPDEPR007</NoWarn>
</PropertyGroup>
```

3. **Migrate to TypedResults**: Migrate to the `TypedResults` pattern documented above to ensure better consistency between application behavior and OpenAPI documentation.

## Affected APIs

- MSBuild property: `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers`
- Associated MVC API analyzers included when `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is `true`
